biology.plasma membrane: The phospholipid bilayer that encloses all cells, regulates transport, and mediates cell signaling.
biology.nucleus: Membrane-bound organelle in eukaryotes that houses the cell's DNA and serves as the site of transcription.
biology.mitochondria: Double-membrane organelles that are the primary site of aerobic ATP production via oxidative phosphorylation.
biology.endoplasmic reticulum: Network of membranes involved in protein synthesis (rough ER) and lipid synthesis and detoxification (smooth ER).
biology.golgi apparatus: Organelle that receives, modifies, sorts, and packages proteins and lipids for secretion or delivery to other organelles.
biology.lysosome: Organelle containing hydrolytic enzymes that degrade macromolecules and cellular debris at low pH.
biology.ribosome: Ribonucleoprotein complex (large and small subunits) that catalyzes translation of mRNA into protein.
biology.cytoskeleton: Network of microfilaments (actin), intermediate filaments, and microtubules that maintains cell shape and enables movement.
biology.osmosis: Passive diffusion of water across a semipermeable membrane from a region of lower solute concentration to higher solute concentration.
biology.active transport: Movement of molecules against their concentration gradient, requiring energy (ATP) and specific carrier proteins.
biology.cell cycle: Ordered sequence of events (G1, S, G2, M) by which a cell grows and divides into two daughter cells.
biology.cytokinesis: Division of the cytoplasm following nuclear division, producing two separate daughter cells.
biology.tight junction: Cell-cell junction that creates an impermeable seal between epithelial cells, preventing paracellular transport.
biology.enzyme: A biological catalyst (usually protein) that accelerates chemical reactions by lowering activation energy without being consumed.
biology.substrate: The specific molecule upon which an enzyme acts, binding at the active site.
biology.km (michaelis constant): The substrate concentration at which reaction velocity is half of Vmax; reflects enzyme-substrate affinity (lower Km = higher affinity).
biology.vmax: The maximum reaction velocity achieved when all enzyme active sites are saturated with substrate.
biology.competitive inhibition: Inhibitor resembles substrate and binds reversibly to the active site; raises apparent Km, Vmax unchanged; overcome by increasing substrate.
biology.allosteric regulation: Regulation of enzyme activity by a molecule binding to a site other than the active site, causing conformational change.
biology.atp: Adenosine triphosphate; the primary cellular energy currency, releasing energy upon hydrolysis of its terminal phosphate bond.
biology.nad+/nadh: Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide; electron carrier that accepts (NAD+ → NADH) or donates electrons (NADH → NAD+) in metabolic reactions.
biology.glycolysis: The ten-step cytoplasmic pathway that converts one glucose into two pyruvate, yielding a net 2 ATP and 2 NADH.
biology.oxidative phosphorylation: The mitochondrial process coupling electron transfer through the ETC to ATP synthesis via ATP synthase, driven by the proton gradient.
biology.beta-oxidation: The mitochondrial process that sequentially cleaves two-carbon acetyl-CoA units from fatty acids, generating NADH, FADH2, and acetyl-CoA.
biology.gluconeogenesis: Synthesis of glucose from non-carbohydrate precursors (pyruvate, lactate, glycerol, amino acids), primarily in the liver.
biology.feedback inhibition: Regulation in which the end product of a metabolic pathway inhibits an earlier enzyme in the same pathway.
biology.dna replication: Semiconservative copying of DNA by DNA polymerase III (prokaryotes) / Pol δ, ε (eukaryotes), always 5'→3', requiring an RNA primer.
biology.transcription: Synthesis of an RNA molecule complementary to the DNA template strand, catalyzed by RNA polymerase.
biology.translation: Ribosomal synthesis of a polypeptide chain from an mRNA template, reading codons 5'→3' and assembling amino acids N→C.
biology.codon: A three-nucleotide sequence in mRNA that specifies one amino acid or a stop signal; the genetic code is read in non-overlapping triplets.
biology.promoter: DNA sequence upstream of a gene to which RNA polymerase and transcription factors bind to initiate transcription.
biology.operon: A unit of prokaryotic gene regulation consisting of a promoter, operator, and co-transcribed structural genes (e.g., the lac operon).
biology.mrna splicing: Post-transcriptional removal of introns and ligation of exons by the spliceosome to produce mature mRNA in eukaryotes.
biology.start codon: AUG; codes for methionine (Met) and defines the start of the open reading frame in translation.
biology.stop codon: UAA, UAG, or UGA; codons that signal termination of translation and release of the polypeptide.
biology.dna polymerase: Enzyme that synthesizes new DNA strands in the 5'→3' direction using a template; requires a primer and has 3'→5' proofreading exonuclease activity.
biology.restriction enzyme: Endonuclease that cleaves double-stranded DNA at specific recognition sequences; used as a tool in recombinant DNA technology.
biology.pcr: Polymerase chain reaction; in vitro method to exponentially amplify a specific DNA region using primers, Taq polymerase, and thermal cycling.
biology.epigenetics: Heritable changes in gene expression that do not involve changes in DNA sequence, mediated by histone modification, DNA methylation, and chromatin remodeling.
biology.allele: One of two or more alternative forms of a gene at a given locus on a chromosome.
biology.genotype: The genetic constitution of an organism at one or more loci.
biology.phenotype: The observable physical or biochemical characteristics of an organism, resulting from genotype and environment.
biology.dominant: An allele whose phenotypic effect is expressed in heterozygotes (one copy sufficient); conventionally denoted by uppercase.
biology.recessive: An allele whose phenotypic effect is expressed only in homozygotes; masked by the dominant allele in heterozygotes.
biology.homozygous: Having two identical alleles at a given locus (AA or aa).
biology.heterozygous: Having two different alleles at a given locus (Aa).
biology.test cross: Cross between an individual of unknown genotype and a homozygous recessive individual to determine the unknown genotype from offspring ratios.
biology.codominance: Both alleles are fully expressed in the heterozygote (e.g., ABO blood type IA and IB alleles).
biology.incomplete dominance: Heterozygote shows an intermediate phenotype between the two homozygotes (e.g., red × white → pink flowers).
biology.epistasis: Interaction between genes at different loci in which one gene modifies or masks the expression of another.
biology.genetic linkage: Tendency of genes located close together on the same chromosome to be inherited together, deviating from independent assortment.
biology.recombination frequency: The proportion of recombinant offspring from a cross; used as a measure of genetic distance (1% recombination = 1 centimorgan).
biology.natural selection: Differential survival and reproduction of individuals with heritable traits that increase fitness in a given environment.
biology.fitness: An organism's reproductive success relative to others; the measure of contribution to the gene pool of the next generation.
biology.adaptation: A heritable trait that increases an organism's fitness in its environment, shaped by natural selection over generations.
biology.genetic drift: Random changes in allele frequencies in a population, most pronounced in small populations; includes bottleneck and founder effects.
biology.gene flow: Transfer of alleles between populations through migration of individuals or gametes; tends to homogenize allele frequencies.
biology.speciation: The evolutionary process by which populations diverge to form reproductively isolated species; allopatric (geographic) and sympatric are the main modes.
biology.reproductive isolation: Barriers (prezygotic or postzygotic) that prevent interbreeding between populations; the defining criterion of biological species.
biology.allopatric speciation: Speciation driven by geographic isolation of populations, allowing independent divergence through selection and drift.
biology.phylogeny: A branching diagram (phylogenetic tree or cladogram) depicting evolutionary relationships among taxa based on shared derived characters.
biology.homology: Structural or molecular similarity between species due to shared ancestry (e.g., vertebrate forelimbs); distinguished from analogy (convergent evolution).
biology.molecular clock: The concept that DNA and protein sequences accumulate mutations at an approximately constant rate, allowing estimation of divergence times.
biology.directional selection: Selection that favors one extreme phenotype, shifting the population mean in one direction.
biology.population: A group of individuals of the same species living in the same area at the same time.
biology.carrying capacity (k): The maximum population size that an environment can sustain indefinitely, given available resources.
biology.exponential growth: Unrestricted population growth at a constant per-capita rate (r), occurring when resources are unlimited (J-shaped curve).
biology.logistic growth: Population growth that slows as density increases due to resource limitation; follows an S-shaped curve, approaching carrying capacity K.
biology.keystone species: A species that has a disproportionately large effect on community structure relative to its abundance (e.g., sea otters controlling urchin populations).
biology.niche: The ecological role and hypervolume of conditions (food, habitat, timing) used by a species; fundamental niche vs. realized niche.
biology.competitive exclusion principle: Two species competing for identical resources cannot coexist indefinitely; one will outcompete and exclude the other.
biology.trophic level: Position in a food chain defined by the number of energy-transfer steps from primary producers: producers, primary consumers, secondary consumers, etc.
biology.biomass pyramid: Diagram showing the total dry mass of organisms at each trophic level; typically decreases at higher levels (~10% energy transfer efficiency).
biology.symbiosis: A close, persistent ecological relationship between two species; types include mutualism, commensalism, and parasitism.
biology.succession: Directional change in community composition over time; primary succession starts on bare substrate; secondary succession follows disturbance of existing community.
biology.net primary productivity (npp): The rate at which producers fix energy minus their own respiration; the energy available to primary consumers.
biology.resting membrane potential: The electrical potential across the plasma membrane of a resting neuron, typically −70 mV, maintained by K⁺ leak channels and the Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase.
biology.action potential: A rapid, all-or-none depolarization and repolarization of the neuronal membrane, propagating along the axon without decrement.
biology.depolarization: Change in membrane potential toward less negative values; occurs when Na⁺ rushes in through voltage-gated Na⁺ channels at threshold (~−55 mV).
biology.synapse: A junction between two neurons (or neuron and effector) where electrical or chemical signals are transmitted.
biology.neurotransmitter: Chemical signal released from a presynaptic terminal that binds to postsynaptic receptors to transmit the nerve signal.
biology.hormone: A chemical messenger secreted by an endocrine gland into the bloodstream that regulates physiology and behavior of distant target cells.
biology.negative feedback: Regulatory mechanism in which a change in a variable triggers a response that opposes and reverses that change, maintaining homeostasis.
biology.epsp/ipsp: Excitatory/inhibitory postsynaptic potential; small depolarizations or hyperpolarizations that summate (temporal and spatial summation) to determine if threshold is reached.
biology.myelin sheath: Lipid-rich insulation formed by oligodendrocytes (CNS) or Schwann cells (PNS) that speeds action potential propagation by saltatory conduction.
biology.nernst potential: The equilibrium membrane potential for a single ion species, at which the electrical gradient exactly opposes the chemical gradient.
biology.hypothalamic-pituitary axis: Central neuroendocrine control hub: hypothalamic releasing/inhibiting hormones regulate anterior pituitary hormone secretion, which controls peripheral endocrine glands.
biology.second messenger: Intracellular signaling molecule (cAMP, IP3, DAG, Ca²⁺) generated in response to hormone-receptor binding, amplifying and transducing the signal.
biology.cardiac output: Volume of blood pumped by the heart per minute: CO = stroke volume × heart rate; normal ~5 L/min at rest.
biology.stroke volume: Volume of blood ejected by the left ventricle per beat; influenced by preload, contractility, and afterload.
biology.hemoglobin: The tetrameric oxygen-transport protein in red blood cells; each subunit carries one heme group that binds one O₂ molecule cooperatively.
biology.oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve: Sigmoidal curve describing hemoglobin O₂ saturation as a function of pO₂; rightward shift (Bohr effect) with ↑CO₂, ↑H⁺, ↑temperature, ↑2,3-BPG.
biology.alveolus: Tiny air sac in the lung across whose thin wall (type I pneumocytes) gas exchange occurs between air and pulmonary capillaries.
biology.glomerular filtration rate (gfr): Volume of plasma filtered by the kidneys per minute (~125 mL/min); a key indicator of kidney function.
biology.nephron: The functional unit of the kidney; consists of the glomerulus, Bowman's capsule, proximal tubule, loop of Henle, distal tubule, and collecting duct.
biology.reabsorption: Recovery of filtered substances (glucose, amino acids, Na⁺, water) from the tubular lumen back into peritubular capillaries.
biology.frank-starling mechanism: The intrinsic ability of the heart to increase stroke volume in response to increased venous return (preload), due to greater sarcomere stretch.
biology.bohr effect: Reduced hemoglobin-O₂ affinity at lower pH and higher CO₂, facilitating O₂ unloading in metabolically active tissues.
biology.renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (ra: Hormonal cascade activated by low blood pressure/Na⁺: renin (kidney) → angiotensin I → ACE → angiotensin II → aldosterone release → Na⁺ and water retention → BP increase.
biology.antidiuretic hormone (adh/vasopressin): Posterior pituitary peptide that increases water reabsorption in the collecting duct by inserting aquaporin-2 channels, concentrating urine.
biology.prokaryote: Single-celled organism lacking a membrane-bound nucleus and organelles; bacteria and archaea; smaller and simpler than eukaryotes.
biology.peptidoglycan: The structural polymer of bacterial cell walls forming a mesh-like layer; thicker in gram-positive, thin outer layer in gram-negative bacteria.
biology.gram stain: Differential staining technique: gram-positive bacteria (thick peptidoglycan) retain crystal violet (purple); gram-negative (thin PG + outer membrane) stain pink with safranin.
biology.binary fission: Asexual reproduction of bacteria in which the cell replicates its chromosome, elongates, and divides into two identical daughter cells.
biology.generation time: The time required for a bacterial population to double; varies from ~20 minutes (E. coli) to hours or days for slow-growing species.
biology.lytic cycle: Viral replication pathway in which the virus hijacks host machinery, replicates, and lyses the host cell to release progeny virions.
biology.lysogenic cycle: Replication pathway in which the viral genome (prophage) integrates into the host chromosome and replicates passively with the host until induced.
biology.plasmid: Small, circular, extrachromosomal DNA element in bacteria that can replicate autonomously and often carries antibiotic resistance genes.
biology.conjugation: Direct transfer of DNA (often a plasmid) between two bacteria via a pilus (sex pilus) and cytoplasmic bridge; requires an F-factor.
biology.antibiotic resistance mechanisms: Bacteria resist antibiotics via: enzymatic inactivation (beta-lactamases), target modification, efflux pumps, or reduced permeability.
biology.endospore: Highly resistant, metabolically dormant structure formed by some gram-positive bacteria (Bacillus, Clostridium) to survive extreme conditions.
biology.capsid: The protein shell surrounding a viral genome; composed of protomeric subunits (capsomers); icosahedral or helical symmetry.
biology.antigen: Any molecule (typically foreign protein) that is recognized by the immune system and can elicit an adaptive immune response.
biology.antibody (immunoglobulin): Y-shaped glycoprotein produced by plasma cells; binds specific antigens via variable regions (Fab); effector functions via constant region (Fc).
biology.b lymphocyte: Adaptive immune cell that matures in bone marrow, expresses B-cell receptor (membrane immunoglobulin), and differentiates into antibody-secreting plasma cells or memory B cells.
biology.t lymphocyte: Adaptive immune cell that matures in the thymus; CD4+ helper T cells coordinate immune responses; CD8+ cytotoxic T cells kill infected cells.
biology.mhc (major histocompatibility complex): Cell-surface proteins that present peptide antigens to T cells; MHC-I (on all nucleated cells, presents to CD8+ T cells) and MHC-II (on APCs, presents to CD4+ T cells).
biology.clonal selection: Process by which an antigen selectively activates the specific lymphocytes bearing complementary receptors, causing them to proliferate (clonal expansion).
biology.innate immunity: Non-specific, immediate first-line defense that does not require prior exposure; includes physical barriers, phagocytes, complement, and pattern-recognition receptors (TLRs).
biology.complement system: Cascade of plasma proteins that opsonize pathogens, promote inflammation, and lyse cells via the membrane attack complex (MAC).
biology.inflammation: Vascular and cellular response to infection or injury characterized by redness, heat, swelling, and pain; driven by cytokines, histamine, and prostaglandins.
biology.opsonization: Coating of a pathogen with antibodies or complement (C3b) to enhance recognition and phagocytosis by macrophages and neutrophils.
biology.cytokine: Small signaling protein secreted by immune cells that regulates immune responses (e.g., interleukins, interferons, TNF-α).
biology.vaccination: Deliberate exposure to an antigen (attenuated pathogen, subunit, mRNA-encoded protein) to prime immunological memory without causing disease.
biology.fertilization: Fusion of a sperm and egg to form a diploid zygote, the single-celled precursor to the entire organism.
biology.cleavage: Rapid mitotic divisions of the zygote without growth, producing the morula and then the blastula/blastocyst.
biology.gastrulation: Morphogenetic process that converts the blastula into a three-layered gastrula (ectoderm, mesoderm, endoderm) through cell migration.
biology.germ layers: Three primary cell layers formed during gastrulation: ectoderm (skin, nervous system), mesoderm (muscle, bone, blood), endoderm (gut lining, lungs, liver).
biology.induction: Process by which one group of cells (organizer) signals neighboring cells to change their developmental fate.
biology.totipotent: Capacity of a cell to develop into any cell type, including extra-embryonic tissues; only the zygote and early blastomeres are totipotent.
biology.pluripotent: Capacity to differentiate into any cell type of the body proper but not extra-embryonic tissues; embryonic stem cells (ESCs) and iPSCs are pluripotent.
biology.homeotic gene: A regulatory gene that specifies body segment identity during development; contains a homeobox encoding the DNA-binding homeodomain.
biology.neural tube: Embryonic precursor to the brain and spinal cord, formed by folding of the neural plate ectoderm; failure to close causes anencephaly or spina bifida.
biology.apoptosis: Programmed cell death involving caspase activation, DNA fragmentation, and cell shrinkage; essential for development (e.g., digit sculpting) and tissue homeostasis.
biology.morphogen gradient: A diffusible signaling molecule whose concentration gradient provides positional information to cells, specifying different cell fates at different thresholds.
biology.anatomical position: Standard reference position: body erect, facing forward, arms at sides with palms facing anteriorly.
biology.sagittal plane: Vertical plane dividing the body into left and right portions; midsagittal (median) plane divides into equal halves.
biology.sarcomere: The basic contractile unit of skeletal muscle, from Z-disk to Z-disk, containing overlapping thick (myosin) and thin (actin) filaments.
biology.sliding filament theory: Muscle contraction mechanism: myosin heads bind actin, undergo power stroke (pivot), shorten the sarcomere by pulling thin filaments toward center; requires ATP and Ca²⁺.
biology.periosteum: The dense connective tissue membrane covering the outer surface of bones; contains osteoblasts, osteoclasts, and blood vessels for remodeling.
biology.synovial joint: Freely movable joint with a synovial cavity, articular cartilage, and synovial membrane secreting lubricating fluid; includes hinge, ball-and-socket types.
biology.dermis: The deep layer of the skin beneath the epidermis; contains collagen, elastin, blood vessels, sweat glands, hair follicles, and nerve endings.
biology.villus (intestinal): Finger-like projection of intestinal mucosa that increases surface area for nutrient absorption; epithelium contains enterocytes with microvilli (brush border).
biology.meninges: Three protective membranes (dura mater, arachnoid mater, pia mater) surrounding the brain and spinal cord; CSF circulates in the subarachnoid space.
biology.lymph node: Secondary lymphoid organ along lymphatic vessels that filters lymph, houses B and T lymphocytes, and initiates adaptive immune responses.
biology.chloroplast: Double-membrane organelle in plant cells that conducts photosynthesis; thylakoid membranes house the light reactions; stroma contains the Calvin cycle enzymes.
biology.photosynthesis: Process by which plants, algae, and cyanobacteria capture light energy to synthesize glucose from CO₂ and H₂O, releasing O₂.
biology.thylakoid: Flattened membranous sac within the chloroplast; stacked into grana; site of the light-dependent reactions and chlorophyll.
biology.stroma: Fluid-filled space of the chloroplast surrounding the thylakoids; site of the Calvin cycle (carbon fixation).
biology.xylem: Vascular tissue that conducts water and dissolved minerals upward from roots; composed of dead tracheids and vessel elements; lignified walls.
biology.phloem: Vascular tissue that transports photosynthate (sucrose) and other organic solutes bidirectionally; composed of living sieve tube elements and companion cells.
biology.transpiration: Evaporative loss of water vapor from leaves through stomata; drives the transpiration-cohesion-tension mechanism for water ascent in xylem.
biology.stomata: Pores in the leaf epidermis surrounded by guard cells that regulate gas exchange (CO₂ in, O₂ and H₂O out) and transpiration.
biology.auxin: Plant hormone (indole-3-acetic acid) promoting cell elongation; responsible for phototropism and gravitropism; produced at shoot apical meristems.
biology.alternation of generations: Plant life cycle alternating between diploid sporophyte (spore-producing) and haploid gametophyte (gamete-producing) generations.
biology.rubisco: Ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate carboxylase/oxygenase; the enzyme that catalyzes carbon fixation in the Calvin cycle; the most abundant protein on Earth.
biology.photorespiration: The wasteful process in which RuBisCO adds O₂ instead of CO₂ to RuBP, consuming energy and releasing CO₂; minimized in C4 and CAM plants.
biology.karyotype: The complete set of chromosomes of a cell, arranged by size and morphology; used to detect chromosomal abnormalities.
biology.aneuploidy: Abnormal chromosome number due to nondisjunction; includes trisomy (47 chromosomes) and monosomy (45 chromosomes).
biology.trisomy 21 (down syndrome): Presence of three copies of chromosome 21; most common viable autosomal trisomy; associated with intellectual disability, characteristic facial features, and heart defects.
biology.autosomal dominant disorder: A disorder caused by a single mutant allele (heterozygous sufficient); parent-to-child transmission (50% risk); each generation affected (e.g., Huntington's, Marfan syndrome).
biology.autosomal recessive disorder: A disorder requiring two mutant alleles; carriers are unaffected; 25% recurrence risk to offspring of two carriers (e.g., cystic fibrosis, sickle cell disease, PKU).
biology.x-linked recessive disorder: Disorder caused by a recessive allele on the X chromosome; primarily affects males (hemizygous); females are carriers; examples: hemophilia A, Duchenne muscular dystrophy.
biology.nondisjunction: Failure of homologous chromosomes or sister chromatids to separate during meiosis (or mitosis), producing aneuploid cells.
biology.genomic imprinting: Epigenetic phenomenon in which gene expression depends on the parent of origin; one allele is silenced by methylation; disruption causes Prader-Willi or Angelman syndrome.
biology.oncogene: A mutated/overexpressed proto-oncogene that drives uncontrolled cell proliferation; acts dominantly (gain-of-function); examples: RAS, MYC, HER2.
biology.tumor suppressor gene: Gene that normally limits cell growth; both alleles must be lost or inactivated to contribute to cancer (two-hit hypothesis); examples: TP53, RB1, BRCA1.
biology.lod score: Logarithm of odds score used in genetic linkage analysis; LOD > 3 considered significant evidence for linkage (1000:1 odds).
biology.penetrance: The proportion of individuals carrying a disease-causing allele who actually manifest the associated phenotype; incomplete penetrance complicates pedigree analysis.
biology.essential nutrient: A nutrient the body cannot synthesize in adequate amounts and must obtain from the diet; includes essential amino acids, fatty acids, vitamins, and minerals.
biology.essential amino acids: The nine amino acids (His, Ile, Leu, Lys, Met, Phe, Thr, Trp, Val) that cannot be synthesized by the human body and must be obtained from diet.
biology.glycemic index: A measure of how rapidly a carbohydrate food raises blood glucose relative to pure glucose (reference = 100); high GI foods cause rapid glucose spikes.
biology.basal metabolic rate (bmr): The minimum energy expenditure required to maintain basic physiological functions at rest in a thermoneutral environment; the largest component of total energy expenditure.
biology.vitamin: Organic micronutrient required in small amounts for normal metabolism; classified as fat-soluble (A, D, E, K) or water-soluble (C, B vitamins).
biology.vitamin d: Fat-soluble vitamin/prohormone; activated form (1,25-dihydroxycholecalciferol, calcitriol) promotes Ca²⁺ and phosphate absorption from gut; deficiency causes rickets (children) or osteomalacia (adults).
biology.iron deficiency anemia: The most common nutritional deficiency worldwide; inadequate iron reduces hemoglobin synthesis; symptoms include fatigue, pallor, microcytic hypochromic red blood cells.
biology.lipoproteins: Protein-lipid complexes that transport cholesterol and triglycerides in the bloodstream; classified by density: chylomicrons, VLDL, IDL, LDL, HDL.
biology.nitrogen balance: The difference between dietary nitrogen intake and excretion; positive balance (growth/pregnancy), negative balance (starvation, illness), zero balance (healthy adult).
biology.ketone bodies: Acetoacetate, beta-hydroxybutyrate, and acetone; produced in the liver from acetyl-CoA during prolonged fasting or carbohydrate restriction; alternative fuel for brain and muscle.
biology.antioxidant: Compound (vitamins C, E; beta-carotene; selenium; polyphenols) that neutralizes reactive oxygen species, reducing oxidative damage to cellular components.

chemistry.mole: The SI unit for amount of substance, equal to 6.02214076e23 elementary entities (Avogadro's number).
chemistry.molar mass: The mass in grams of one mole of a substance, numerically equal to its formula mass in atomic mass units.
chemistry.limiting reagent: The reactant that is completely consumed first in a reaction, thereby determining the maximum yield of product.
chemistry.theoretical yield: The maximum mass of product predicted by stoichiometry from the limiting reagent.
chemistry.percent yield: The ratio of actual yield to theoretical yield expressed as a percentage.
chemistry.empirical formula: The simplest whole-number ratio of atoms of each element in a compound.
chemistry.molarity (m): The number of moles of solute dissolved per liter of solution.
chemistry.isotope: Atoms of the same element with the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons, and hence different mass numbers.
chemistry.atomic mass unit (amu): A unit of mass equal to 1/12 the mass of a carbon-12 atom, approximately 1.66054e-27 kg.
chemistry.percent composition: The mass percentage of each element in a compound, calculated as (element mass / molar mass) x 100.
chemistry.ideal gas: A hypothetical gas whose particles have no intermolecular forces and negligible volume, obeying PV = nRT exactly.
chemistry.partial pressure: The pressure a gas would exert if it alone occupied the total volume at the same temperature.
chemistry.mole fraction: The ratio of moles of one component to total moles in a mixture; chi_i = n_i / n_total.
chemistry.root-mean-square speed: The square root of the mean of the squared molecular speeds, equal to sqrt(3RT/M).
chemistry.effusion: The passage of a gas through a tiny orifice into a vacuum; rate inversely proportional to sqrt(molar mass).
chemistry.van der waals constants: Empirical parameters a (accounts for intermolecular attractions) and b (accounts for molecular volume) in the van der Waals real-gas equation.
chemistry.compressibility factor z: The ratio PV/(nRT); equals 1 for an ideal gas and deviates from 1 for real gases.
chemistry.standard temperature and pressure (stp): Defined by IUPAC as 0 degrees C (273.15 K) and 100 kPa; molar volume of ideal gas = 22.711 L/mol.
chemistry.mean free path: The average distance a gas molecule travels between successive collisions.
chemistry.maxwell-boltzmann distribution: The statistical distribution of molecular speeds in an ideal gas at thermal equilibrium.
chemistry.internal energy (u): The total microscopic energy of a system; changes by dU = q + w (heat added plus work done on the system).
chemistry.enthalpy (h): A state function defined as H = U + PV; at constant pressure, delta-H equals heat transferred.
chemistry.entropy (s): A state function measuring the dispersal of energy; for a reversible process dS = dq_rev / T.
chemistry.gibbs free energy (g): G = H - TS; a decrease in G at constant T and P indicates a spontaneous process.
chemistry.helmholtz free energy (a): A = U - TS; a decrease in A at constant T and V indicates spontaneity; equal to reversible work.
chemistry.standard state: A reference condition: pure substance at 1 bar (solutes at 1 mol/L activity), specified temperature (usually 298.15 K).
chemistry.hess's law: The enthalpy change of a reaction equals the algebraic sum of enthalpies of any sequence of steps connecting reactants to products.
chemistry.heat capacity (c): The amount of heat required to raise the temperature of a substance by one kelvin; C_P = (dH/dT)_P, C_V = (dU/dT)_V.
chemistry.chemical potential (mu): The partial molar Gibbs free energy of a species; mu_i = (dG/dn_i)_{T,P,n_j}.
chemistry.third law of thermodynamics: The entropy of a perfect crystalline substance is zero at absolute zero temperature (0 K).
chemistry.van't hoff enthalpy: The enthalpy change of a process derived from the temperature dependence of the equilibrium constant via d(ln K)/d(1/T) = -delta_H/R.
chemistry.kirchhoff's law: The temperature dependence of reaction enthalpy: d(delta_H)/dT = delta_C_P.
chemistry.equilibrium constant (k): The ratio of product activities to reactant activities, each raised to stoichiometric coefficients, at equilibrium.
chemistry.k_c: Equilibrium constant expressed in terms of molar concentrations.
chemistry.k_p: Equilibrium constant expressed in terms of partial pressures (in bar or atm as specified).
chemistry.reaction quotient (q): The ratio of product to reactant concentrations (or pressures) at any point; if Q < K reaction proceeds forward.
chemistry.le chatelier's principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a stress by shifting in the direction that partially offsets that stress.
chemistry.solubility product (k_sp): The equilibrium constant for the dissolution of a sparingly soluble ionic compound into its constituent ions.
chemistry.common-ion effect: The decrease in solubility of an ionic compound caused by the presence of a common ion already in solution.
chemistry.ice table: An Initial-Change-Equilibrium table used to track concentration changes during an equilibrium calculation.
chemistry.degree of dissociation (alpha): The fraction of a substance that has dissociated at equilibrium.
chemistry.heterogeneous equilibrium: An equilibrium involving reactants or products in more than one phase; pure solids and liquids are omitted from the K expression.
chemistry.bronsted-lowry acid: A species that donates a proton (H+) to another species.
chemistry.bronsted-lowry base: A species that accepts a proton (H+) from another species.
chemistry.lewis acid: A species that accepts an electron pair from a donor; broader than the Bronsted-Lowry definition.
chemistry.conjugate acid-base pair: Two species differing by a single proton: the acid donates H+ to give the conjugate base.
chemistry.k_a: Acid dissociation constant: K_a = [A-][H+] / [HA] for weak acid HA.
chemistry.k_w: Ion-product constant of water: K_w = [H+][OH-] = 1.01e-14 at 25 degrees C.
chemistry.ph: The negative base-10 logarithm of the hydrogen-ion activity (approximately [H+] in dilute aqueous solution).
chemistry.buffer: A solution that resists changes in pH upon addition of small amounts of acid or base, typically a weak acid and its conjugate base.
chemistry.henderson-hasselbalch equation: pH = pK_a + log([A-]/[HA]); used to calculate pH of buffer solutions.
chemistry.equivalence point: The point in a titration at which the moles of titrant added are stoichiometrically equal to the moles of analyte.
chemistry.half-equivalence point: The point in a weak-acid titration at which [HA] = [A-] and therefore pH = pK_a.
chemistry.buffer capacity: The amount of acid or base a buffer can absorb per unit pH change; maximized when pH = pK_a.
chemistry.rate law: An expression relating the rate of a reaction to the concentrations of reactants: rate = k[A]^m[B]^n.
chemistry.rate constant (k): The proportionality constant in the rate law; depends on temperature and the specific reaction but not on concentration.
chemistry.reaction order: The sum of the exponents in the rate law (overall order); the exponent for each reactant is the order in that reactant.
chemistry.half-life (t_1/2): The time required for the concentration of a reactant to decrease to half its initial value.
chemistry.activation energy (e_a): The minimum energy that colliding molecules must possess for a reaction to occur; from the Arrhenius equation.
chemistry.arrhenius pre-exponential factor (a): The frequency factor in the Arrhenius equation; related to the frequency of properly oriented collisions.
chemistry.transition state: The highest-energy configuration along the reaction coordinate, corresponding to the top of the activation-energy barrier.
chemistry.elementary step: A single molecular event in a mechanism; its molecularity equals the order of that step.
chemistry.rate-determining step: The slowest step in a reaction mechanism, which limits the overall reaction rate.
chemistry.michaelis-menten kinetics: A kinetic model for enzyme catalysis: v = V_max[S] / (K_M + [S]), where K_M is the Michaelis constant.
chemistry.steady-state approximation: The assumption that the concentration of a reactive intermediate remains nearly constant throughout the reaction.
chemistry.oxidation state: A bookkeeping convention assigning hypothetical charge to an atom based on electronegativity rules in bonding.
chemistry.standard electrode potential (e^o): The potential of a half-cell under standard conditions (1 M concentration, 1 bar partial pressure, 25 degrees C) relative to the SHE.
chemistry.standard hydrogen electrode (she): The reference half-cell with E^o defined as 0 V: H+(aq, 1M) + e- <=> 1/2 H2(g, 1 bar).
chemistry.emf (e_cell): The electromotive force (cell potential) driving current in a galvanic cell; E_cell = E_cathode - E_anode.
chemistry.nernst equation: Relates cell potential to the reaction quotient Q: E = E^o - (RT/nF) ln Q.
chemistry.faraday's constant (f): The charge of one mole of electrons, equal to 96485 C/mol.
chemistry.anode: The electrode at which oxidation occurs; in a galvanic cell it is the negative terminal.
chemistry.cathode: The electrode at which reduction occurs; in a galvanic cell it is the positive terminal.
chemistry.overpotential: The additional voltage beyond the thermodynamic minimum required to drive an electrolytic reaction at a given rate.
chemistry.concentration cell: A galvanic cell whose EMF arises solely from a difference in concentration of the same species at the two electrodes.
chemistry.wavefunction (psi): A mathematical function whose square |psi|^2 gives the probability density for finding a particle at a given point.
chemistry.quantum number n: The principal quantum number (n = 1, 2, 3, ...); determines the energy and size of the orbital.
chemistry.quantum number l: The azimuthal (angular momentum) quantum number (0 to n-1); determines orbital shape (s, p, d, f for l = 0, 1, 2, 3).
chemistry.quantum number m_l: The magnetic quantum number (-l to +l); specifies orbital orientation in space.
chemistry.heisenberg uncertainty principle: The position and momentum of a particle cannot both be known exactly: delta_x * delta_p >= h/(4*pi).
chemistry.de broglie wavelength: The wavelength associated with a particle of momentum p: lambda = h/p.
chemistry.aufbau principle: Electrons fill orbitals in order of increasing energy, following the (n + l) rule.
chemistry.hund's rule: Electrons occupy orbitals of equal energy singly with parallel spins before pairing.
chemistry.pauli exclusion principle: No two electrons in an atom can have the same four quantum numbers; each orbital holds at most two electrons with opposite spin.
chemistry.effective nuclear charge (z_eff): The net positive charge experienced by an electron after accounting for shielding by other electrons: Z_eff = Z - sigma.
chemistry.ionization energy (ie): The minimum energy needed to remove an electron from a gaseous atom or ion in its ground state.
chemistry.electron affinity (ea): The energy change when an electron is added to a neutral gaseous atom in its ground state.
chemistry.bond order: In MO theory, (bonding electrons - antibonding electrons) / 2; higher bond order means shorter and stronger bond.
chemistry.vsepr: Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion theory; molecular geometry determined by minimizing repulsion between electron domains around the central atom.
chemistry.hybridization: The mixing of atomic orbitals to form new hybrid orbitals of equal energy directed toward bonding partners.
chemistry.dipole moment (mu): A measure of charge separation in a molecule: mu = q * d; units are debye (D) or C·m.
chemistry.hydrogen bond: A strong electrostatic interaction between a hydrogen atom covalently bonded to a highly electronegative atom (N, O, F) and a lone pair on another such atom.
chemistry.lattice energy: The energy released when gaseous ions come together to form an ionic crystal; related to ionic charges and radii via the Born-Lande equation.
chemistry.formal charge: A bookkeeping charge assigned to an atom in a Lewis structure: formal charge = (valence electrons) - (lone-pair electrons) - 0.5*(bonding electrons).
chemistry.resonance: The description of a molecule or ion as a hybrid of two or more Lewis structures (resonance structures) that differ only in electron placement.
chemistry.electronegativity: A measure of an atom's ability to attract bonding electrons to itself; increases across a period and decreases down a group (Pauling scale).
chemistry.london dispersion force: A transient induced-dipole/induced-dipole attraction present between all molecules; strength increases with polarizability and molecular mass.
chemistry.nucleophile: An electron-pair donor that attacks electrophilic centers (electron-poor atoms); Lewis base.
chemistry.electrophile: An electron-pair acceptor that attacks nucleophilic centers; Lewis acid.
chemistry.carbocation: A species bearing a positively charged trivalent carbon; stability order: tertiary > secondary > primary > methyl.
chemistry.carbanion: A species bearing a negatively charged carbon; stability increased by adjacent electron-withdrawing groups.
chemistry.sn2 mechanism: Bimolecular nucleophilic substitution proceeding through a backside-attack transition state with inversion of configuration; rate = k[Nu][substrate].
chemistry.sn1 mechanism: Unimolecular nucleophilic substitution through a carbocation intermediate; rate depends only on substrate concentration; can give racemization.
chemistry.markovnikov's rule: In electrophilic addition to an unsymmetrical alkene, the electrophile (H+) adds to the carbon with more hydrogen atoms.
chemistry.zaitsev's rule: Elimination reactions preferentially give the more substituted (more stable) alkene product.
chemistry.enantiotopic / diastereotopic: Groups that are related by symmetry operations: enantiotopic replacements give enantiomers; diastereotopic replacements give diastereomers.
chemistry.resonance stabilization: Delocalization of electrons over multiple atoms, stabilizing a species relative to a localized structure.
chemistry.diels-alder reaction: A [4+2] cycloaddition between a conjugated diene (4 pi electrons) and a dienophile (2 pi electrons) to form a six-membered ring; syn-addition, endo rule.
chemistry.enol / enolate: An enol is a tautomer of a carbonyl compound with OH on a sp2 carbon; an enolate is its conjugate base, a resonance-stabilized carbanion.
chemistry.beer-lambert law: Absorbance A = epsilon * c * l, where epsilon is molar absorptivity, c is molar concentration, and l is path length.
chemistry.transmittance (t): The fraction of light that passes through a sample: T = I/I_0; A = -log10(T).
chemistry.chemical shift (delta): In NMR, the resonance frequency of a nucleus relative to a reference (TMS), expressed in parts per million (ppm).
chemistry.spin-spin coupling constant (j): The NMR coupling constant in Hz between neighboring nuclei, related to dihedral angle by the Karplus equation.
chemistry.molar absorptivity (epsilon): The intrinsic property of a substance relating absorbance to concentration and path length; units are L/(mol·cm).
chemistry.fingerprint region: The complex region of an IR spectrum from ~500–1500 cm-1 unique to each molecule; used for compound identification.
chemistry.molecular ion (m+): In mass spectrometry, the intact molecule that has lost one electron; its m/z equals the molecular weight.
chemistry.base peak: The most abundant ion peak in a mass spectrum, set to 100% relative abundance.
chemistry.bragg's law: The condition for constructive interference in X-ray diffraction: n*lambda = 2*d*sin(theta).
chemistry.selection rule: A quantum-mechanical rule specifying allowed transitions; for IR, a vibration must cause a change in dipole moment; for Raman, a change in polarizability.
chemistry.coordination compound: A compound containing a central metal atom or ion bonded to surrounding ligands via coordinate (dative) bonds.
chemistry.ligand: An ion or molecule that donates electron pairs to a central metal atom; classified by denticity (mono-, bi-, polydentate).
chemistry.crystal field splitting (delta): The energy gap between the lower and higher d-orbital sets in a ligand field; Delta_oct > Delta_tet for same ligands.
chemistry.high-spin / low-spin: Configuration of d electrons when Delta is less than or greater than the pairing energy, respectively; affects magnetic properties.
chemistry.spectrochemical series: An ordering of ligands by their ability to split d orbitals: I- < Br- < Cl- < F- < OH- < H2O < NH3 < en < CO < CN-.
chemistry.chelate effect: The enhanced thermodynamic stability of complexes formed by polydentate ligands compared to analogous monodentate ligand complexes, driven by entropy.
chemistry.18-electron rule: Stable transition-metal organometallic compounds tend to have 18 electrons in the metal valence shell (filled bonding MOs).
chemistry.crystal field stabilization energy (cfse: The energy gained by a complex due to preferential occupation of lower-energy d orbitals in a ligand field.
chemistry.trans effect: The ability of a ligand to labilize (facilitate substitution of) the ligand trans to it; strongest for high-field ligands (CO, CN-, H-).
chemistry.oxidative addition: A reaction in which a metal inserts into a bond (X-Y), increasing the metal's oxidation state by 2 and coordination number by 2.
chemistry.partition function (q or z): The sum over all quantum states of the Boltzmann factor: q = sum_i exp(-E_i / k_B T); encodes all thermodynamic information of a system.
chemistry.boltzmann distribution: The probability of a system being in state i: P_i = exp(-E_i / k_B T) / q.
chemistry.canonical ensemble: A statistical mechanical ensemble of systems at fixed N, V, and T; the partition function is Q = q^N / N! for distinguishable or indistinguishable particles.
chemistry.equipartition theorem: Each quadratic term in the classical Hamiltonian contributes (1/2)k_B T to the mean energy; translational and rotational modes each contribute (3/2)k_B T (linear), vibrational at high T contributes k_B T per mode.
chemistry.residual entropy: Non-zero entropy remaining at 0 K due to degeneracy in the ground state (e.g., CO, ice); S_0 = k_B ln(W).
chemistry.symmetry number (sigma): The number of equivalent orientations of a molecule obtained by rotations; used to avoid overcounting in the rotational partition function.
chemistry.zero-point energy (zpe): The energy (1/2)*h*nu_e of the lowest vibrational quantum level; non-zero even at absolute zero.
chemistry.microcanonical ensemble: An ensemble of isolated systems at fixed N, V, and E; entropy is S = k_B ln(Omega) where Omega is the number of microstates.
chemistry.grand canonical ensemble: An ensemble at fixed mu (chemical potential), V, and T; useful for open systems where particle number fluctuates.
chemistry.helmholtz free energy from q: A = -k_B T ln(Q); all other thermodynamic functions derived from A by standard relations.
chemistry.born-oppenheimer approximation: The assumption that electrons adjust instantaneously to nuclear positions because electrons are much lighter; separates nuclear and electronic wavefunctions.
chemistry.variational principle: The expectation value of the Hamiltonian for any trial wavefunction is greater than or equal to the true ground-state energy: <psi|H|psi> >= E_0.
chemistry.hartree-fock (hf) method: A mean-field SCF method that approximates the many-electron wavefunction as a single Slater determinant of spin-orbitals; neglects electron correlation.
chemistry.basis set: A finite set of mathematical functions (atomic orbitals) used to expand molecular orbitals in quantum chemistry calculations.
chemistry.electron correlation energy: The difference between the exact non-relativistic energy and the Hartree-Fock energy: E_corr = E_exact - E_HF; always negative.
chemistry.density functional theory (dft): A method that replaces the many-electron wavefunction with the electron density rho(r) as the fundamental variable, based on the Hohenberg-Kohn theorems.
chemistry.exchange-correlation functional: The unknown functional E_xc[rho] in DFT that accounts for exchange (Pauli) and correlation effects; approximated by LDA, GGA, hybrid functionals.
chemistry.coupled cluster (cc) theory: A high-accuracy post-HF method that expresses the wavefunction as exp(T)|HF>; CCSD(T) is considered the 'gold standard' for molecular energetics.
chemistry.slater determinant: An antisymmetrized product of spin-orbitals that automatically satisfies the Pauli exclusion principle for fermions.
chemistry.potential energy surface (pes): The electronic energy as a function of nuclear coordinates; stationary points correspond to equilibrium geometries and transition states.
chemistry.amino acid: A molecule containing an amino group (-NH2), a carboxyl group (-COOH), a hydrogen, and a side chain (R group) attached to the alpha carbon.
chemistry.peptide bond: An amide bond formed between the alpha-carboxyl group of one amino acid and the alpha-amino group of another with loss of water; has partial double-bond character due to resonance.
chemistry.isoelectric point (pi): The pH at which an amino acid (or protein) bears no net charge; pI = average of the pKa values flanking the zwitterion form.
chemistry.michaelis constant (k_m): The substrate concentration at which the reaction rate is half of V_max; reflects the affinity of the enzyme for its substrate (lower K_M = higher affinity).
chemistry.competitive inhibition: Inhibition in which the inhibitor competes with the substrate for the enzyme active site; increases apparent K_M, V_max unchanged.
chemistry.allosteric regulation: Regulation of an enzyme by binding of a molecule (effector) at a site other than the active site, inducing a conformational change that alters activity.
chemistry.atp (adenosine triphosphate): The primary energy currency of cells; hydrolysis of the terminal phosphoanhydride bond releases approximately -30.5 kJ/mol under standard biological conditions.
chemistry.glycolysis: The anaerobic metabolic pathway that converts one glucose (6C) to two pyruvate (3C) molecules, producing a net of 2 ATP and 2 NADH.
chemistry.dna double helix: The antiparallel, right-handed B-form double helix of DNA; adenine pairs with thymine (2 H-bonds), guanine pairs with cytosine (3 H-bonds).
chemistry.phospholipid bilayer: The fundamental structure of biological membranes; amphipathic phospholipids arrange with hydrophilic headgroups facing aqueous phases and hydrophobic tails forming the interior.
chemistry.coenzyme: A non-protein organic molecule that assists an enzyme; examples include NAD+, FAD, coenzyme A, and ATP.
chemistry.vmax: The maximum velocity of an enzymatic reaction at saturating substrate concentration; equals k_cat times total enzyme concentration.
chemistry.activity (a): The effective concentration of a species accounting for non-ideal interactions: a_i = gamma_i * [i] / c^o (c^o = 1 M standard).
chemistry.activity coefficient (gamma): A dimensionless factor relating activity to molarity; approaches 1 at infinite dilution; decreases with increasing ionic strength.
chemistry.ionic strength (i): A measure of the total ion concentration in solution: I = (1/2) * sum(c_i * z_i^2) for all ions i.
chemistry.debye-huckel limiting law: log(gamma) = -A * z^2 * sqrt(I) for dilute solutions; A = 0.509 in water at 25 degrees C.
chemistry.conditional formation constant (k_f'): An effective formation constant for a metal-EDTA complex at a specified pH, corrected for competing protonation equilibria of EDTA: K_f' = alpha_Y4- * K_f.
chemistry.theoretical plate (n): A measure of chromatographic column efficiency: N = (t_R / sigma)^2 or N = 16(t_R/W)^2.
chemistry.resolution (r_s): A measure of chromatographic separation: R_s = 2(t_R2 - t_R1) / (W_1 + W_2); R_s >= 1.5 for baseline resolution.
chemistry.van deemter equation: H = A + B/u + C*u, where H is the height equivalent to a theoretical plate, u is the mobile-phase velocity, A=eddy diffusion, B=longitudinal diffusion, C=mass transfer.
chemistry.standard addition method: A calibration technique for complex matrices in which known amounts of analyte are added to the sample solution to correct for matrix effects.
chemistry.propagation of uncertainty: The method for calculating the uncertainty in a result derived from uncertain measurements; for sums/differences, absolute uncertainties add in quadrature; for products/quotients, relative uncertainties add in quadrature.

computer_science.amortized analysis: Method of analyzing algorithm cost averaged over a sequence of operations, smoothing out costly outliers (e.g., dynamic array doubling gives O(1) amortized push).
computer_science.avl tree: Self-balancing BST where for every node the heights of the two child subtrees differ by at most 1; guarantees O(log n) search, insert, delete.
computer_science.red-black tree: BST with one extra bit per node encoding color (red/black) and four structural invariants that guarantee O(log n) height.
computer_science.b-tree: Balanced search tree where each node can hold multiple keys and have multiple children; designed to minimize disk I/O, used in databases and file systems.
computer_science.hash collision: Two distinct keys mapping to the same bucket; resolved by chaining (linked lists) or open addressing (linear/quadratic probing, double hashing).
computer_science.heap property: In a max-heap, every node's key is ≥ its children's keys; in a min-heap, ≤; guarantees O(1) peek at extremum.
computer_science.load factor: Ratio of stored elements to hash table capacity (n/m); kept below a threshold (e.g., 0.75) to maintain O(1) expected operations.
computer_science.trie: Tree where each node represents a prefix of stored strings; supports O(L) lookup, insert, and prefix search for strings of length L.
computer_science.graph adjacency list: Graph representation storing, for each vertex, a list of its neighbors; space O(V+E), preferred for sparse graphs.
computer_science.disjoint-set (union-find): Data structure tracking a partition of elements into disjoint sets; supports union and find operations, nearly O(1) amortized with union-by-rank + path compression.
computer_science.p: Class of decision problems solvable by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time.
computer_science.np: Class of decision problems for which a YES-certificate can be verified by a deterministic TM in polynomial time; equivalently, solvable by a nondeterministic TM in polynomial time.
computer_science.np-complete: A problem X is NP-complete if X ∈ NP and every problem in NP is polynomial-time reducible to X; intuitively, the hardest problems in NP.
computer_science.np-hard: A problem to which every NP problem reduces in polynomial time; need not itself be in NP (e.g., HALT is NP-hard but undecidable).
computer_science.polynomial-time reduction: A transformation f computable in poly-time such that x ∈ A iff f(x) ∈ B; written A ≤_p B; used to show hardness.
computer_science.dynamic programming: Algorithm design paradigm breaking problems into overlapping subproblems, solving each once, and storing results (memoization or bottom-up table) to avoid redundant recomputation.
computer_science.greedy algorithm: Algorithm that makes the locally optimal choice at each step; correct when a greedy-choice property and optimal substructure hold (proven e.g. by exchange argument).
computer_science.amortized complexity: Per-operation cost averaged over a sequence; potential method assigns a potential Φ to data structure states: amortized cost = actual cost + ΔΦ.
computer_science.approximation ratio: For a minimization problem, an algorithm has ratio ρ if it always returns a solution ≤ ρ · OPT; analogously for maximization.
computer_science.recurrence relation: Equation defining the running time T(n) recursively in terms of smaller inputs, e.g. T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n).
computer_science.pspace: Class of decision problems solvable by a deterministic TM using polynomial space; P ⊆ NP ⊆ PSPACE.
computer_science.co-np: Class of problems whose complements are in NP; a problem is in co-NP if NO-instances have polynomial-time verifiable certificates.
computer_science.dfa (deterministic finite automaton): 5-tuple (Q, Σ, δ, q0, F): states Q, alphabet Σ, transition function δ: Q×Σ→Q, start state q0, accept states F; recognizes exactly the regular languages.
computer_science.nfa (nondeterministic finite automaton): Like a DFA but δ: Q×Σ_ε → P(Q); accepts if any computation path reaches an accept state; equivalent in power to DFA (subset construction converts).
computer_science.regular language: A language recognized by some DFA; equivalently described by a regular expression; closed under union, concatenation, Kleene star, complement, intersection.
computer_science.pumping lemma (regular): For any regular language L, ∃ pumping length p such that any string s ∈ L with |s| ≥ p can be split s=xyz with |xy|≤p, |y|≥1, and xy^iz ∈ L for all i≥0; used to prove non-regularity.
computer_science.context-free grammar (cfg): 4-tuple (V, Σ, R, S): variables V, terminals Σ, production rules R of form A→α, start variable S; generates context-free languages.
computer_science.pushdown automaton (pda): NFA augmented with a stack; recognizes exactly the context-free languages; can be nondeterministic.
computer_science.pumping lemma (cfl): For any CFL L, ∃ p such that any s ∈ L with |s| ≥ p can be split s=uvxyz with |vy|≥1, |vxy|≤p, and uv^ixy^iz ∈ L for all i≥0; used to prove non-context-freeness.
computer_science.turing machine (tm): 7-tuple (Q, Σ, Γ, δ, q0, q_accept, q_reject) operating on an infinite tape; the canonical model of computation; computes exactly the recursively enumerable (RE) languages.
computer_science.decidable language: A language for which there exists a TM that always halts and correctly accepts or rejects every input; also called recursive.
computer_science.recognizable language: A language for which some TM halts and accepts every string in the language, but may loop on strings not in it; also called recursively enumerable (RE).
computer_science.halting problem: HALT_TM = {⟨M,w⟩ | TM M halts on input w}; undecidable (proven by diagonalization); RE but not co-RE.
computer_science.reduction (computability): A mapping reduction A ≤_m B: a computable function f where x∈A iff f(x)∈B; if B is decidable then so is A; contrapositively, if A is undecidable then B is undecidable.
computer_science.rice's theorem: Any nontrivial semantic property of Turing machine languages is undecidable; i.e., no algorithm can decide whether the language of an arbitrary TM has a nontrivial property.
computer_science.tautology: A propositional formula that is true under every truth assignment; e.g., p ∨ ¬p.
computer_science.satisfiability: A formula is satisfiable if there exists at least one truth assignment making it true; SAT is the canonical NP-complete problem.
computer_science.mathematical induction: Proof technique: establish base case P(0), then show P(k) → P(k+1) for all k ≥ 0; concludes P(n) for all n ≥ 0.
computer_science.strong induction: Assume P(0), P(1), ..., P(k) all hold, then prove P(k+1); equivalent in power to ordinary induction.
computer_science.bijection: A function that is both injective (one-to-one) and surjective (onto); establishes that two sets have the same cardinality.
computer_science.cantor's diagonalization: Proof that the reals are uncountable: for any enumeration of reals, construct a real differing from the n-th real in the n-th decimal place.
computer_science.modular arithmetic: Arithmetic on residues mod n; a ≡ b (mod n) iff n | (a-b); forms the ring Z/nZ.
computer_science.euler's totient φ(n): Count of integers in [1,n] coprime to n; φ(p) = p-1 for prime p; φ(mn) = φ(m)φ(n) if gcd(m,n)=1.
computer_science.pigeonhole principle: If n+1 objects are placed into n boxes, at least one box contains ≥ 2 objects; generalized: if kn+1 objects in n boxes, some box has ≥ k+1.
computer_science.graph isomorphism: Graphs G and H are isomorphic if there is a bijection f: V(G)→V(H) preserving adjacency; not known to be in P or NP-complete.
computer_science.euler path/circuit: Path/circuit visiting every edge exactly once; exists in undirected graph iff 0 (path) or 0 vertices (circuit) have odd degree.
computer_science.planar graph: Graph embeddable in the plane without edge crossings; by Euler's formula V - E + F = 2 for connected planar graphs.
computer_science.process: A program in execution; has its own address space, PC, registers, open files, and OS state; isolated from other processes.
computer_science.thread: Lightweight execution unit within a process; shares address space and file descriptors with other threads of the same process; has its own stack and registers.
computer_science.context switch: OS operation saving the CPU state of a running process/thread and loading the state of another; overhead measured in microseconds.
computer_science.semaphore: Synchronization primitive with integer value; P (wait/down) decrements and blocks if <0; V (signal/up) increments and wakes a blocked process; invented by Dijkstra.
computer_science.mutex (mutual exclusion lock): Binary semaphore used to ensure only one thread executes a critical section at a time; provides ownership semantics.
computer_science.deadlock: Circular wait among processes where each holds a resource another needs; necessary conditions (Coffman): mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, circular wait.
computer_science.banker's algorithm: Deadlock avoidance algorithm by Dijkstra; grants resource requests only if remaining allocation leaves the system in a safe state (where all processes can finish sequentially).
computer_science.virtual memory: Abstraction giving each process a private large address space implemented by mapping virtual pages to physical frames; enables demand paging and isolation.
computer_science.page fault: Trap raised when a process accesses a virtual page not currently in physical memory; OS handles by loading the page from disk (demand paging).
computer_science.tlb (translation lookaside buffer): Hardware cache for recent virtual-to-physical address translations; hit avoids page table walk; managed by OS on a TLB miss (software-managed) or hardware.
computer_science.inode: Unix file system data structure storing file metadata (permissions, timestamps, size, pointers to data blocks) but not the filename.
computer_science.round-robin scheduling: CPU scheduling where each process gets a fixed time quantum in circular order; preemptive; fair but causes many context switches if quantum is too small.
computer_science.osi model: 7-layer reference model: Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application; each layer provides services to the one above.
computer_science.tcp (transmission control protocol): Connection-oriented, reliable, ordered byte-stream transport protocol; uses three-way handshake, sequence numbers, ACKs, and flow/congestion control.
computer_science.udp (user datagram protocol): Connectionless, unreliable transport protocol with minimal overhead; no handshake, ordering, or retransmission; used for DNS, video streaming, gaming.
computer_science.ip address: 32-bit (IPv4) or 128-bit (IPv6) network-layer identifier for a host or interface; hierarchically structured to enable routing.
computer_science.cidr (classless inter-domain routing): IP address allocation using variable-length subnet masks written as a.b.c.d/prefix; aggregates routes and reduces routing table size.
computer_science.bgp (border gateway protocol): Path-vector routing protocol used between autonomous systems on the Internet; makes routing decisions based on paths, network policies, and rule sets.
computer_science.ospf (open shortest path first): Link-state intra-domain routing protocol; each router floods its link-state advertisements; runs Dijkstra to compute shortest paths.
computer_science.tcp three-way handshake: Connection establishment: client sends SYN, server replies SYN-ACK, client sends ACK; establishes sequence numbers for reliable delivery.
computer_science.congestion window (cwnd): TCP sender-side limit on unacknowledged bytes; increased in slow start (exponentially) and congestion avoidance (linearly); halved on packet loss.
computer_science.dns (domain name system): Hierarchical distributed database mapping hostnames to IP addresses; resolvers query root, TLD, and authoritative name servers; uses UDP port 53.
computer_science.tls (transport layer security): Cryptographic protocol providing authentication, confidentiality, and integrity over TCP; uses asymmetric crypto for key exchange, symmetric for bulk encryption.
computer_science.nat (network address translation): Maps multiple private IP addresses to a single public IP using port numbers; breaks end-to-end connectivity; ubiquitous in IPv4 networks.
computer_science.relation (table): A set of tuples over a fixed schema (attribute names and domains); the fundamental structure in the relational model.
computer_science.functional dependency: X → Y holds if any two tuples agreeing on attributes X must agree on attributes Y; basis for normalization theory.
computer_science.bcnf (boyce-codd normal form): For every non-trivial FD X → Y, X must be a superkey; eliminates all redundancy from FDs; may not preserve all dependencies.
computer_science.3nf (third normal form): For every non-trivial FD X → A, either X is a superkey or A is prime (part of some key); always achieves lossless join + dependency-preserving decomposition.
computer_science.acid: Transaction properties: Atomicity (all-or-nothing), Consistency (valid state → valid state), Isolation (concurrent transactions appear serial), Durability (committed changes survive crashes).
computer_science.serializable schedule: A concurrent transaction schedule equivalent to some serial execution of the same transactions; the gold standard for correctness.
computer_science.two-phase locking (2pl): Concurrency control protocol: transactions acquire all locks before releasing any (growing phase then shrinking phase); guarantees serializability.
computer_science.mvcc (multiversion concurrency control): Each write creates a new version; readers access a consistent snapshot without blocking writers; used in PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB, Oracle.
computer_science.b+ tree index: Variant of B-tree where all data pointers are in leaves (linked for range scans) and internal nodes hold only routing keys; standard database index structure.
computer_science.query plan: The execution strategy chosen by the query optimizer, expressed as a tree of relational operators (scan, join, sort); cost estimated using statistics and cardinality.
computer_science.cap theorem: A distributed system can guarantee at most two of: Consistency, Availability, and Partition tolerance; formalized by Brewer and proven by Gilbert & Lynch.
computer_science.wal (write-ahead log): Durability mechanism: all changes are logged to stable storage before being applied to the data pages; enables crash recovery and REDO/UNDO.
computer_science.isa (instruction set architecture): The interface between hardware and software defining the set of instructions, registers, addressing modes, data types, and memory model a processor implements.
computer_science.risc: Reduced Instruction Set Computer: simple, fixed-length instructions, large register file, load-store architecture, hardwired control; examples: MIPS, ARM, RISC-V.
computer_science.cisc: Complex Instruction Set Computer: variable-length instructions, memory operands, complex addressing modes, microcode; example: x86.
computer_science.pipeline hazard: Condition preventing the next instruction from executing in its expected clock cycle; types: structural (resource conflict), data (RAW/WAW/WAR dependency), control (branch).
computer_science.raw (read-after-write) hazard: True data dependency: an instruction reads a register before a preceding instruction has written it; resolved by forwarding or stalling.
computer_science.cache hit/miss: A hit occurs when requested data is found in cache (fast); a miss requires fetching from a slower memory level; miss types: compulsory, capacity, conflict.
computer_science.cache locality: Temporal locality: recently accessed data likely reaccessed soon; spatial locality: data near recently accessed data likely accessed soon; justifies caching.
computer_science.direct-mapped cache: Each memory block maps to exactly one cache line (index = address mod #lines); simple but prone to conflict misses.
computer_science.set-associative cache: Cache with k lines per set; a block maps to one set, can occupy any of k lines; k-way SA balances hit rate and hardware cost.
computer_science.amat (average memory access time): AMAT = hit time + miss rate × miss penalty; fundamental metric for cache design.
computer_science.branch prediction: Processor mechanism to guess the outcome of a branch before it is resolved; misprediction causes pipeline flush (penalty = pipeline depth).
computer_science.out-of-order execution: CPU executes instructions as operands become available rather than in program order; uses register renaming (Tomasulo algorithm) to avoid false dependencies.
computer_science.lexer (scanner): Compiler phase converting a character stream into a token stream using regular expressions / finite automata; recognizes identifiers, literals, operators, keywords.
computer_science.parser: Compiler phase constructing an abstract syntax tree from a token stream; verifies syntactic correctness; based on CFG; outputs an AST.
computer_science.ll(k) grammar: Top-down parseable grammar using k lookahead tokens; FIRST and FOLLOW sets determine parse table; LL(1) is the most common.
computer_science.lr(k) grammar: Bottom-up parseable grammar using k lookahead tokens; more powerful than LL(k); variants: SLR, LALR(1) (used in yacc/bison), canonical LR(1).
computer_science.ast (abstract syntax tree): Tree representation of program structure after parsing; omits syntactic sugar; basis for semantic analysis, type checking, and code generation.
computer_science.type system: Set of rules assigning types to expressions; a sound type system guarantees no type errors at runtime for well-typed programs (progress + preservation).
computer_science.static vs dynamic typing: Static: types checked at compile time (Java, C, Haskell); dynamic: types checked at runtime (Python, JavaScript); gradual typing mixes both.
computer_science.ssa (static single assignment): IR where each variable is assigned exactly once; uses φ-functions at merge points; simplifies many optimizations (constant propagation, dead code elimination).
computer_science.dataflow analysis: Compile-time analysis propagating information along control flow graph edges to determine program properties; examples: live variables, reaching definitions, constant folding.
computer_science.register allocation: Mapping of unlimited virtual registers to finite physical registers; modeled as graph coloring (interference graph); NP-complete in general, heuristics used.
computer_science.garbage collection: Automatic memory management reclaiming unreachable objects; algorithms: reference counting, mark-and-sweep, copying, generational GC.
computer_science.closure: First-class function paired with its captured environment (free variables); enables higher-order programming; implemented via heap-allocated environment record.
computer_science.bias-variance tradeoff: Test error = bias² + variance + irreducible noise; high bias = underfitting (model too simple); high variance = overfitting (model too complex); regularization and ensembles help.
computer_science.gradient descent: Iterative optimization: θ ← θ − η∇L(θ); η is the learning rate; variants: SGD (one example), mini-batch, momentum, Adam.
computer_science.backpropagation: Efficient computation of gradients in a neural network using the chain rule applied backward through the computation graph; O(forward pass) time.
computer_science.overfitting: Model memorizes training data, failing to generalize; diagnosed by high train accuracy but high test error; addressed by regularization, dropout, data augmentation, early stopping.
computer_science.regularization: Adding a penalty to the loss to discourage complex models; L2 (ridge): +λ||w||², shrinks weights; L1 (lasso): +λ||w||_1, produces sparsity.
computer_science.svm (support vector machine): Classifier maximizing the margin between classes; decision boundary defined by support vectors; kernel trick enables nonlinear boundaries implicitly in high-dimensional spaces.
computer_science.kernel trick: Replaces dot products ⟨x,z⟩ with a kernel function K(x,z) = ⟨φ(x),φ(z)⟩, enabling nonlinear classifiers without explicitly computing high-dimensional feature maps.
computer_science.em algorithm: Expectation-Maximization: iteratively computes expected log-likelihood (E-step) and maximizes it over parameters (M-step); converges to local maximum; used for GMMs, HMMs.
computer_science.pac learning: Probably Approximately Correct: a concept class is PAC-learnable if there is an algorithm that with probability ≥ 1-δ outputs a hypothesis with error ≤ ε using poly(1/ε, 1/δ, n) samples.
computer_science.vc dimension: Largest set of points that a hypothesis class can shatter (classify in all 2^m ways); higher VC dimension = more expressive but more data needed for generalization.
computer_science.attention mechanism: Computes a weighted sum of value vectors, weights determined by compatibility between query and key vectors; enables Transformers to model long-range dependencies in O(n²) time.
computer_science.cross-entropy loss: L = −Σ_i y_i log(p_i); standard loss for classification; equivalent to negative log-likelihood under a categorical distribution; minimized by gradient descent.
computer_science.softmax function: σ(z)_i = exp(z_i) / Σ_j exp(z_j); maps logit vector to probability distribution; numerically stabilized by subtracting max(z).
computer_science.bayes' theorem: P(A|B) = P(B|A)·P(A) / P(B); relates conditional and marginal probabilities; foundation of Bayesian inference.
computer_science.expectation: E[X] = Σ x·P(X=x) (discrete) or ∫x·f(x)dx (continuous); linearity: E[aX+bY] = aE[X] + bE[Y] always (no independence required).
computer_science.variance: Var(X) = E[(X−E[X])²] = E[X²] − (E[X])²; Var(aX+b) = a²Var(X); Var(X+Y) = Var(X)+Var(Y) if X,Y independent.
computer_science.law of large numbers: Sample mean X̄_n → E[X] as n→∞ in probability (weak LLN) or almost surely (strong LLN); basis for Monte Carlo estimation.
computer_science.central limit theorem: √n(X̄_n − μ) / σ → N(0,1) in distribution as n→∞; sample means of i.i.d. finite-variance variables are approximately Gaussian.
computer_science.markov chain: Sequence of random variables where P(X_{n+1}=j | X_n, ..., X_0) = P(X_{n+1}=j | X_n); memoryless; characterized by transition matrix P.
computer_science.stationary distribution: Distribution π such that πP = π; for ergodic chains, π is unique and the chain converges to π from any starting state.
computer_science.mle (maximum likelihood estimation): θ̂_MLE = argmax_θ Σ log p(x_i; θ); finds parameter maximizing probability of observed data; consistent and asymptotically efficient under regularity conditions.
computer_science.p-value: Probability of observing a test statistic at least as extreme as observed, assuming the null hypothesis is true; small p-value (< α) → reject H_0.
computer_science.poisson distribution: P(X=k) = λ^k e^{-λ}/k!; models count of rare events in fixed interval; E[X]=Var(X)=λ; Poisson approximates Binomial when n large, p small, λ=np.
computer_science.kvl (kirchhoff's voltage law): The sum of voltages around any closed loop in a circuit equals zero; a consequence of energy conservation.
computer_science.kcl (kirchhoff's current law): The sum of currents entering any node equals the sum of currents leaving; a consequence of charge conservation.
computer_science.thevenin equivalent: Any linear two-terminal network can be replaced by a voltage source V_th in series with a resistance R_th; V_th = open-circuit voltage, R_th = resistance with independent sources zeroed.
computer_science.norton equivalent: Any linear two-terminal network can be replaced by a current source I_N in parallel with R_N; I_N = short-circuit current, R_N = R_th; I_N = V_th / R_th.
computer_science.superposition: In a linear circuit with multiple independent sources, the response is the sum of responses due to each source acting alone (others zeroed).
computer_science.capacitor: Element storing energy in electric field; V = (1/C)∫i dt; i = C·dV/dt; impedance Z_C = 1/(jωC) in frequency domain; blocks DC, passes AC.
computer_science.inductor: Element storing energy in magnetic field; V = L·di/dt; i = (1/L)∫V dt; impedance Z_L = jωL; blocks AC, passes DC.
computer_science.time constant τ: RC: τ = RC; RL: τ = L/R; time for exponential response to reach 1−1/e ≈ 63.2% of final value; system response ∝ exp(−t/τ).
computer_science.op-amp: Operational amplifier: high-gain differential voltage amplifier; ideal: infinite input impedance, zero output impedance, infinite open-loop gain; used with negative feedback for precise circuits.
computer_science.impedance: Z = R + jX; generalization of resistance to AC circuits; Z_R = R; Z_C = 1/(jωC) = −j/(ωC); Z_L = jωL; Ohm's law: V = IZ in phasor domain.
computer_science.power factor: cos(θ) where θ is the phase angle between voltage and current; PF=1 for purely resistive load; PF<1 for reactive loads; P = V_rms·I_rms·PF.
computer_science.lti system: Linear Time-Invariant system: satisfies superposition (linearity) and output doesn't depend on absolute time (time-invariance); fully characterized by its impulse response h(t).
computer_science.convolution: (f * g)(t) = ∫_{-∞}^{∞} f(τ)g(t−τ)dτ; output of LTI system: y(t) = x(t) * h(t); discrete: y[n] = Σ_k x[k]h[n−k].
computer_science.impulse response h(t): Output of an LTI system when input is the Dirac delta δ(t); any LTI system output = input convolved with h(t).
computer_science.fourier transform: X(jω) = ∫_{-∞}^{∞} x(t)e^{−jωt}dt; maps signal from time domain to frequency domain; inverse: x(t) = (1/2π)∫X(jω)e^{jωt}dω.
computer_science.laplace transform: X(s) = ∫_0^{∞} x(t)e^{−st}dt; generalizes Fourier transform to complex s = σ+jω; converts differential equations to algebraic equations.
computer_science.transfer function h(s): H(s) = Y(s)/X(s) for LTI system with zero initial conditions; ratio of Laplace transforms of output and input; encodes poles and zeros.
computer_science.nyquist-shannon sampling theorem: A bandlimited signal with maximum frequency f_max can be perfectly reconstructed from samples taken at f_s ≥ 2·f_max (Nyquist rate); sampling below causes aliasing.
computer_science.aliasing: Distortion when a signal is sampled below the Nyquist rate; high-frequency components appear as lower-frequency imposters; prevented by anti-aliasing (low-pass) filter before sampling.
computer_science.z-transform: X(z) = Σ_{n=−∞}^{∞} x[n]z^{−n}; discrete-time analog of Laplace transform; H(z) = Y(z)/X(z) for DT-LTI systems.
computer_science.dtft (discrete-time fourier transform): X(e^{jω}) = Σ_{n=−∞}^{∞} x[n]e^{−jωn}; frequency representation of discrete-time signals; periodic with period 2π.
computer_science.bode plot: Log-frequency plots of magnitude |H(jω)| in dB = 20log10|H(jω)| and phase ∠H(jω) vs log(ω); asymptotic approximations use ±20dB/decade per pole/zero.
