computer_science.open-loop control: Control system where the output is not measured or fed back; simpler but sensitive to disturbances and model errors.
computer_science.closed-loop (feedback) control: Control system where the output is measured and compared to the reference; error drives the controller; reduces sensitivity to disturbances and plant variations.
computer_science.transfer function g(s): G(s) = Y(s)/U(s) for a linear system; encodes dynamics as ratio of polynomials in s; poles = roots of denominator, zeros = roots of numerator.
computer_science.pole: Value of s where transfer function goes to infinity (root of denominator); determines system modes; poles with Re(s) < 0 → stable mode; Re(s) > 0 → unstable.
computer_science.bibo stability: Bounded-input bounded-output stability: system is BIBO stable iff all poles have negative real parts; equivalently, impulse response is absolutely integrable.
computer_science.routh-hurwitz criterion: Algebraic test for stability: construct Routh array from characteristic polynomial coefficients; system is stable iff all entries in first column are positive.
computer_science.root locus: Locus of closed-loop poles as gain K varies from 0 to ∞; starts at open-loop poles (K=0), ends at open-loop zeros or infinity; designed for transient response shaping.
computer_science.pid controller: Controller with Proportional (K_p·e), Integral (K_i∫e dt), and Derivative (K_d·de/dt) terms; P reduces error, I eliminates steady-state error, D improves transient response.
computer_science.gain margin: How much open-loop gain can increase before instability; GM = 1/|G(jω_pc)| in linear scale, where ω_pc is phase crossover frequency (∠G = -180°).
computer_science.phase margin: Additional phase lag at gain crossover frequency (|G(jω_gc)|=1) before instability; PM = 180° + ∠G(jω_gc); good design: GM > 6 dB, PM > 30-45°.
computer_science.steady-state error: Error as t→∞ for a given reference input; e_ss = lim_{s→0} s·E(s) by Final Value Theorem; depends on system type (number of pure integrators in open-loop).
computer_science.boolean algebra: Algebraic structure with two elements {0,1}, operations AND (·), OR (+), NOT (¬); satisfies commutativity, associativity, distributivity, identity, complement, and absorption laws.
computer_science.minterm: A product term in which every variable appears exactly once (complemented or not); minterm m_i corresponds to the binary representation of i; SOP form = sum of minterms.
computer_science.maxterm: A sum term in which every variable appears exactly once; POS form = product of maxterms; maxterm M_i corresponds to minterms with the same index.
computer_science.sop (sum of products): Boolean expression as OR of AND terms; canonical SOP = sum of all minterms where function = 1; can be implemented with two-level AND-OR logic.
computer_science.karnaugh map (k-map): Grid-based minimization tool for Boolean functions; adjacent cells differ by one variable; groupings of 1, 2, 4, 8... cells give prime implicants.
computer_science.combinational logic: Logic circuit whose outputs depend only on current inputs, not history; no feedback or state; implemented with gates; examples: adder, MUX, decoder.
computer_science.sequential logic: Logic circuit with memory/state; outputs depend on inputs AND current state; implemented with flip-flops; examples: counters, registers, FSMs.
computer_science.d flip-flop: Edge-triggered storage element; Q follows D on rising clock edge; Q+ = D; used for registers and pipelines.
computer_science.mealy machine: FSM where outputs depend on both current state and current inputs; output can change mid-state; generally fewer states than equivalent Moore machine.
computer_science.moore machine: FSM where outputs depend only on current state; outputs change only on state transitions; more predictable, used in most hardware FSM designs.
computer_science.don't-care condition: Input combination that never occurs or for which output is irrelevant; can be assigned 0 or 1 to simplify Boolean expression; marked X in K-maps.
computer_science.hazard (glitch): Momentary incorrect output in combinational logic due to different propagation delays along paths; static hazards (brief wrong constant), dynamic hazards (multiple transitions).
computer_science.p-n junction: Interface between p-type and n-type semiconductor; built-in potential V_bi = (kT/q)·ln(N_A·N_D/n_i²); under forward bias, current flows exponentially; reverse bias, small leakage.
computer_science.diode equation (shockley): I = I_0(e^{V/(nV_T)} − 1); I_0 = reverse saturation current; n = ideality factor (1-2); V_T = kT/q ≈ 26 mV at room temperature.
computer_science.bjt (bipolar junction transistor): Three-terminal device (Base, Collector, Emitter); in active mode: I_C = β·I_B; I_E = (1+β)·I_B; controlled current amplifier; β (h_FE) typically 50-300.
computer_science.mosfet: Metal-oxide-semiconductor field-effect transistor; gate voltage controls channel; n-channel NMOS: I_D = μ_n C_ox (W/L)[(V_GS−V_th)V_DS − V_DS²/2] (triode); I_D = (μ_n C_ox W/2L)(V_GS−V_th)² (saturation).
computer_science.threshold voltage v_th: Gate voltage at which MOSFET channel inverts and current begins to flow; depends on oxide thickness, doping, and substrate bias.
computer_science.cmos (complementary mos): Logic family using pairs of NMOS and PMOS transistors; NMOS pulls output low, PMOS pulls output high; no DC current in steady state → low static power; standard for digital ICs.
computer_science.noise margin: NM_H = V_OH − V_IH; NM_L = V_IL − V_OL; measure of robustness against noise; CMOS: NM ≈ V_DD/2 for symmetric gates.
computer_science.transconductance g_m: g_m = ∂I_D/∂V_GS = 2I_D/(V_GS−V_th) for MOSFET in saturation; g_m = I_C/V_T for BJT; key small-signal parameter relating input voltage to output current.
computer_science.small-signal model: Linear approximation of transistor behavior around a DC operating point (Q-point); MOSFET: controlled current source g_m·v_gs with output resistance r_o = |V_A|/I_D; valid for small signal swings.
computer_science.dynamic power (cmos): P_dynamic = α·C·V_DD²·f; α = activity factor (0-1), C = load capacitance, V_DD = supply voltage, f = clock frequency; dominant power consumption in digital ICs.

economics.demand curve: A schedule or curve showing the quantity of a good consumers are willing and able to purchase at each price, ceteris paribus; slopes downward due to the law of demand.
economics.supply curve: A schedule or curve showing the quantity producers are willing and able to sell at each price, ceteris paribus; slopes upward due to increasing marginal costs.
economics.equilibrium price: The price at which quantity demanded equals quantity supplied, clearing the market with no surplus or shortage.
economics.surplus (excess supply): A market condition in which quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded at the prevailing price, placing downward pressure on price.
economics.shortage (excess demand): A market condition in which quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied at the prevailing price, placing upward pressure on price.
economics.price ceiling: A legally imposed maximum price; causes a shortage when set below equilibrium price.
economics.price floor: A legally imposed minimum price; causes a surplus when set above equilibrium price.
economics.consumer surplus: The difference between the maximum a consumer is willing to pay and the price actually paid, representing welfare gain to buyers.
economics.producer surplus: The difference between the price a producer receives and the minimum price at which the producer would supply the good.
economics.ceteris paribus: Latin for 'all else equal'; the assumption that all variables other than those under study remain constant.
economics.normal good: A good for which demand increases as consumer income rises.
economics.inferior good: A good for which demand decreases as consumer income rises.
economics.price elasticity of demand (ped): The percentage change in quantity demanded divided by the percentage change in price; measures responsiveness of demand to price changes.
economics.elastic demand: Demand is elastic when |PED| > 1; quantity demanded is highly responsive to price changes.
economics.inelastic demand: Demand is inelastic when |PED| < 1; quantity demanded is not very responsive to price changes.
economics.unit elastic: Demand is unit elastic when |PED| = 1; total revenue is unchanged when price changes.
economics.income elasticity of demand (yed): The percentage change in quantity demanded divided by the percentage change in income; positive for normal goods, negative for inferior goods.
economics.cross-price elasticity: The percentage change in quantity demanded of good X divided by the percentage change in price of good Y; positive for substitutes, negative for complements.
economics.price elasticity of supply (pes): The percentage change in quantity supplied divided by the percentage change in price; always non-negative.
economics.perfectly inelastic: Supply or demand is perfectly inelastic when elasticity equals zero; the curve is vertical.
economics.perfectly elastic: Supply or demand is perfectly elastic when elasticity is infinite; the curve is horizontal.
economics.arc elasticity: Elasticity calculated using midpoint (average) values of price and quantity over an interval, avoiding end-point sensitivity.
economics.utility: A numerical representation of a consumer's preferences over bundles of goods; higher utility indicates preferred bundles.
economics.marginal utility (mu): The additional utility gained from consuming one more unit of a good, holding other consumption constant; typically diminishing.
economics.indifference curve: A curve showing all combinations of goods that yield equal utility; slopes downward and is convex to the origin under standard assumptions.
economics.marginal rate of substitution (mrs): The rate at which a consumer is willing to trade one good for another while remaining on the same indifference curve; equals -MU_x/MU_y.
economics.budget constraint: The set of consumption bundles affordable at given income and prices: P_x·X + P_y·Y = I.
economics.marshallian demand: Ordinary (uncompensated) demand that maximizes utility subject to the budget constraint; depends on prices and income.
economics.hicksian demand: Compensated demand that minimizes expenditure while achieving a given utility level; isolates the pure substitution effect.
economics.substitution effect: The change in quantity demanded due solely to the change in relative prices, holding utility constant.
economics.income effect: The change in quantity demanded due to the change in real purchasing power when a price changes.
economics.slutsky equation: Decomposes the total price effect into substitution and income effects: ∂x/∂p = ∂h/∂p - x·(∂x/∂I).
economics.giffen good: An inferior good with such a large income effect that demand curves upward; quantity demanded rises when price rises.
economics.expenditure function: The minimum expenditure required to achieve a given utility level at given prices: e(p,u).
economics.indirect utility function: The maximum utility achievable given prices and income: V(p,I).
economics.production function: A function Q = f(K,L) relating the quantity of output to the quantities of capital and labor (or other inputs) used.
economics.marginal product of labor (mpl): The additional output produced by employing one more unit of labor, holding capital constant: MPL = ∂Q/∂L.
economics.marginal rate of technical substitution: The rate at which capital can be substituted for labor while holding output constant; equals MPL/MPK along an isoquant.
economics.isoquant: A curve showing all combinations of inputs that produce a given quantity of output.
economics.returns to scale: How output changes when all inputs are scaled proportionally: constant (CRS), increasing (IRS), or decreasing (DRS).
economics.total cost (tc): The sum of all costs incurred in production: TC = TFC + TVC.
economics.average total cost (atc): Total cost per unit of output: ATC = TC/Q.
economics.marginal cost (mc): The incremental cost of producing one more unit of output: MC = ΔTC/ΔQ = dTC/dQ.
economics.sunk cost: A cost already incurred and unrecoverable regardless of future decisions; should not influence forward-looking choices.
economics.economies of scale: A situation in which long-run average cost declines as output increases.
economics.diseconomies of scale: A situation in which long-run average cost rises as output increases.
economics.economies of scope: Cost savings achieved when it is cheaper to produce two goods jointly than separately: C(Q1,Q2) < C(Q1,0) + C(0,Q2).
economics.isocost line: A line showing all combinations of inputs that can be hired at a given total expenditure: wL + rK = C.
economics.perfect competition: A market structure with many price-taking firms, homogeneous products, free entry/exit, and perfect information.
economics.price taker: A firm that takes the market price as given because it is too small to influence it; faces a perfectly elastic demand curve.
economics.monopoly: A market with a single seller of a product with no close substitutes, giving the firm market power over price.
economics.market power: The ability of a firm to profitably set price above marginal cost.
economics.lerner index: A measure of market power: L = (P - MC)/P = -1/|E_d|; ranges from 0 (perfect competition) to 1 (pure monopoly).
economics.price discrimination: Charging different prices to different customers for the same good, exploiting differences in willingness to pay.
economics.cournot competition: An oligopoly model in which firms simultaneously choose quantities, taking rivals' quantities as fixed.
economics.bertrand competition: An oligopoly model in which firms simultaneously set prices; with homogeneous goods and equal costs, results in P = MC.
economics.stackelberg competition: An oligopoly model in which a leader firm moves first, choosing quantity, and a follower then best-responds.
economics.monopolistic competition: A market with many firms selling differentiated products, with free entry driving long-run profits to zero but P > MC.
economics.natural monopoly: A market in which a single firm can serve the entire demand at lower cost than two or more firms, due to economies of scale.
economics.herfindahl-hirschman index (hhi): A measure of market concentration: HHI = Σ s_i^2 where s_i is each firm's market share in percentage; ranges from 0 to 10,000.
economics.pareto efficiency: An allocation is Pareto efficient if no one can be made better off without making someone else worse off.
economics.pareto improvement: A reallocation that makes at least one person better off without making anyone worse off.
economics.externality: A cost or benefit that affects a third party not involved in the market transaction, causing market failure.
economics.negative externality: A cost imposed on third parties by market participants; leads to overproduction relative to the social optimum.
economics.positive externality: A benefit conferred on third parties; leads to underproduction relative to the social optimum.
economics.pigouvian tax: A corrective tax set equal to the marginal external cost, internalizing the externality and restoring social efficiency.
economics.social marginal cost: Private marginal cost plus marginal external cost: SMC = PMC + MEC.
economics.coase theorem: If property rights are well-defined and transaction costs are zero, parties will negotiate to the efficient outcome regardless of the initial rights assignment.
economics.public good: A good that is both non-excludable (cannot prevent non-payers from consuming) and non-rival (one person's consumption does not reduce another's).
economics.free rider problem: The tendency for individuals to under-contribute to public goods because they can benefit without paying.
economics.common resource: A good that is non-excludable but rival in consumption; subject to overuse (tragedy of the commons).
economics.deadweight loss: The loss in total surplus from a market distortion (tax, externality, market power) relative to the efficient outcome.
economics.nash equilibrium: A strategy profile in which no player can increase their payoff by unilaterally deviating, given the strategies of all other players.
economics.dominant strategy: A strategy that yields a higher payoff than any other strategy regardless of what opponents play.
economics.mixed strategy: A probability distribution over pure strategies; a player randomizes to keep opponents indifferent.
economics.subgame perfect equilibrium (spe): A Nash equilibrium in which players' strategies constitute a Nash equilibrium in every subgame; found by backward induction.
economics.backward induction: A method for solving extensive-form games by starting at the terminal nodes and working backward to determine optimal choices.
economics.prisoner's dilemma: A symmetric two-player game in which the dominant strategy for each player leads to a collectively suboptimal outcome.
economics.coordination game: A game in which players benefit from choosing matching strategies; typically has multiple Nash equilibria.
economics.pareto dominant equilibrium: A Nash equilibrium that yields higher payoffs to all players than any other Nash equilibrium.
economics.repeated game: A game in which the same stage game is played multiple times; repetition can sustain cooperation via punishment strategies.
economics.folk theorem: In infinitely repeated games with sufficient patience, any individually rational feasible outcome can be sustained as a subgame perfect equilibrium.
economics.bayesian nash equilibrium: A Nash equilibrium in a game of incomplete information in which each player maximizes expected payoff given beliefs about types.
economics.auction (second-price): A Vickrey auction in which the highest bidder wins and pays the second-highest bid; truth-telling is a dominant strategy.
economics.gdp (gross domestic product): The market value of all final goods and services produced within a country's borders in a given period.
economics.nominal gdp: GDP measured at current market prices without adjusting for inflation.
economics.real gdp: GDP adjusted for price changes using a base-year price level; reflects actual changes in output volume.
economics.gdp deflator: A price index equal to (Nominal GDP / Real GDP) × 100; measures the overall price level of domestically produced goods.
economics.cpi (consumer price index): A price index measuring the cost of a fixed basket of goods and services purchased by a typical urban consumer.
economics.gnp (gross national product): The market value of all final goods and services produced by a country's residents, regardless of location: GNP = GDP + Net factor income from abroad.
economics.net exports (nx): The value of exports minus imports; a component of GDP equal to X - M.
economics.national saving: The total income in the economy not spent on consumption or government purchases: S = Y - C - G.
economics.investment (i): Expenditure on new capital goods, residential construction, and changes in business inventories.
economics.value added: The increase in the value of a good or service at each stage of production; GDP equals total value added across all sectors.
economics.is curve: The locus of (Y, r) pairs at which the goods market is in equilibrium (planned expenditure equals output); slopes downward in (Y, r) space.
economics.lm curve: The locus of (Y, r) pairs at which the money market is in equilibrium (money supply equals money demand); slopes upward in (Y, r) space.
economics.multiplier: The ratio by which output changes in response to an autonomous spending change: k = 1/(1-MPC) in a simple Keynesian model.
economics.crowding out: The reduction in private investment resulting from government borrowing that raises interest rates; reduces the effectiveness of fiscal expansion.
economics.liquidity trap: A situation in which interest rates are at or near zero, making monetary policy ineffective because people prefer to hold cash rather than bonds.
economics.aggregate demand (ad): The total demand for final goods and services in an economy at each price level; slopes downward due to the wealth, interest rate, and exchange rate effects.
economics.short-run aggregate supply (sras): The total supply of goods and services at each price level in the short run when nominal wages and prices of some inputs are sticky; slopes upward.
economics.long-run aggregate supply (lras): The total supply of goods and services at the natural rate of output (full employment); vertical in (Y, P) space.
economics.stagflation: The simultaneous occurrence of high inflation and high unemployment, typically caused by a negative supply shock.
economics.natural rate of output: The level of output consistent with full employment and the long-run equilibrium of the economy (potential GDP).
economics.mpc (marginal propensity to consume): The fraction of an additional dollar of income that households spend on consumption.
economics.m1 money supply: The most liquid measure of money including currency in circulation and demand deposits (checkable deposits).
economics.m2 money supply: A broader money measure including M1 plus savings deposits, small time deposits, and money market mutual funds.
economics.money multiplier: The ratio of the money supply to the monetary base: m = 1/rr (in simple model) or m = (1+c)/(rr+c) with currency holdings.
economics.monetary base (high-powered money): Currency in circulation plus bank reserves; directly controlled by the central bank.
economics.reserve ratio: The fraction of deposits that banks hold as reserves; can be required (required reserve ratio) or held voluntarily (excess reserves).
economics.open market operations: Central bank purchases or sales of government securities to expand or contract the monetary base.
economics.nominal interest rate: The interest rate expressed in monetary terms, unadjusted for inflation.
economics.real interest rate: The nominal interest rate minus the inflation rate (or expected inflation): r ≈ i - π.
economics.quantity theory of money: The theory that the price level is proportional to the money supply: MV = PY, implying inflation equals money growth minus output growth.
economics.seigniorage: Revenue earned by the government from money creation; the real value of resources acquired by printing money.
economics.taylor rule: A monetary policy guideline: i = r* + π + α(π - π*) + β(Y - Y*); the central bank raises interest rates when inflation exceeds target or output exceeds potential.
economics.velocity of money: The average number of times a dollar is spent on final goods in a period: V = PY/M.
economics.unemployment rate: The percentage of the labor force that is not employed but actively seeking work: u = U/(U+E) × 100%.
economics.labor force: The sum of employed and unemployed persons who are of working age and actively seeking employment.
economics.frictional unemployment: Temporary unemployment arising from the time taken to match workers with suitable jobs; reflects normal labor market turnover.
economics.structural unemployment: Unemployment caused by a mismatch between workers' skills and the skills required by available jobs, often due to technological change or sectoral shifts.
economics.cyclical unemployment: Unemployment caused by insufficient aggregate demand during economic downturns; the gap between actual and natural unemployment.
economics.natural rate of unemployment (u*): The unemployment rate consistent with stable inflation; equal to frictional plus structural unemployment.
economics.nairu: Non-Accelerating Inflation Rate of Unemployment; the unemployment rate at which inflation is stable; synonymous with natural rate in standard models.
economics.phillips curve: An empirical inverse relationship between unemployment and inflation; the short-run Phillips curve shifts with expected inflation.
economics.okun's law: An empirical relationship between output gaps and cyclical unemployment: a 1 percentage point rise in unemployment corresponds to approximately a 2% fall in output below potential.
economics.efficiency wage: A wage above the market-clearing level set by firms to boost worker productivity, reduce turnover, or attract better workers; can cause unemployment.
economics.labor force participation rate: The percentage of the civilian non-institutional population that is in the labor force.
economics.solow model: A neoclassical growth model in which the long-run growth rate is determined by exogenous technological progress; capital accumulation drives transitional dynamics.
economics.capital per worker (k): The capital-labor ratio: k = K/L; the central state variable in the Solow model.
economics.steady state: The long-run equilibrium in the Solow model where capital per worker is constant (investment equals depreciation plus population growth).
economics.depreciation: The rate at which capital wears out or becomes obsolete, typically denoted δ.
economics.golden rule capital stock: The steady-state capital-per-worker level that maximizes consumption per worker: MPK = δ + n + g.
economics.total factor productivity (tfp): The portion of output growth not explained by input growth; a measure of technological efficiency: A in Y = A·F(K,L).
economics.solow residual: The empirical measure of TFP growth, calculated as output growth minus the weighted sum of capital and labor growth.
economics.conditional convergence: The tendency for economies with similar structural parameters to converge to the same steady state; poorer economies grow faster given identical fundamentals.
economics.endogenous growth: Growth theory in which the long-run growth rate is explained by the model itself (e.g., through R&D, human capital, or knowledge spillovers) rather than exogenous TFP.
economics.growth accounting: A technique decomposing output growth into contributions from capital, labor, and TFP: ΔY/Y = α·ΔK/K + (1-α)·ΔL/L + ΔA/A.
economics.discretionary fiscal policy: Deliberate changes in government spending or taxes to stabilize the economy, requiring explicit policy decisions.
economics.automatic stabilizers: Budget mechanisms that automatically increase spending or reduce taxes in recessions (e.g., unemployment insurance, progressive tax) without new legislation.
economics.ricardian equivalence: The proposition that tax cuts financed by debt have no effect on aggregate demand because rational consumers save the tax cut in anticipation of future higher taxes.
economics.government budget constraint: G - T = ΔB + ΔM; the government deficit must be financed by issuing bonds or money.
economics.primary deficit: The government deficit excluding interest payments on existing debt: G - T (excluding interest).
economics.debt-to-gdp ratio dynamics: The change in the debt-to-GDP ratio depends on the primary deficit and the difference between the real interest rate and the growth rate.
economics.inflation targeting: A monetary policy framework in which the central bank announces an explicit inflation target and adjusts policy instruments to achieve it.
economics.time inconsistency: The tendency for policymakers to deviate from previously announced optimal plans once the private sector has formed expectations; motivates central bank independence and rules.
economics.quantitative easing (qe): An unconventional monetary policy in which the central bank purchases large quantities of assets (beyond short-term government bonds) to lower long-term interest rates and stimulate the economy.
economics.ols (ordinary least squares): A method of estimating regression coefficients by minimizing the sum of squared residuals.
economics.regression coefficient (β): The estimated change in the dependent variable associated with a one-unit increase in an independent variable, holding other variables constant.
economics.residual: The difference between the observed and fitted value of the dependent variable: e_i = y_i - ŷ_i.
economics.r² (coefficient of determination): The proportion of variance in the dependent variable explained by the regression: R² = 1 - SSR/SST; ranges from 0 to 1.
economics.adjusted r²: R² adjusted for the number of regressors: R̄² = 1 - [(1-R²)(n-1)/(n-k-1)]; penalizes for additional variables.
economics.t-statistic: The ratio of an estimated coefficient to its standard error: t = β̂/SE(β̂); used to test whether a coefficient is statistically different from a hypothesized value.
economics.f-statistic: A test statistic for the joint significance of multiple coefficients; tests whether all slope coefficients are simultaneously zero.
economics.p-value: The probability of observing a test statistic at least as extreme as the one calculated, under the null hypothesis; a small p-value (< 0.05) rejects the null.
economics.omitted variable bias: Bias in OLS estimates when a variable that affects the dependent variable and is correlated with included regressors is excluded from the regression.
economics.heteroskedasticity: A violation of OLS assumption in which the variance of the error term varies across observations; OLS remains unbiased but is no longer efficient, and standard errors are incorrect.
economics.multicollinearity: A condition in which two or more independent variables are highly correlated, making it difficult to estimate their individual coefficients precisely.
economics.gauss-markov theorem: Under classical OLS assumptions (linearity, exogeneity, homoskedasticity, no serial correlation), OLS is BLUE: Best Linear Unbiased Estimator.
economics.standard error: The estimated standard deviation of an estimator; measures the precision of the coefficient estimate.
economics.endogeneity: A condition in which a regressor is correlated with the error term, causing OLS to be biased and inconsistent; arises from omitted variables, reverse causality, or measurement error.
economics.instrumental variable (iv): A variable that is correlated with an endogenous regressor (relevance) but uncorrelated with the error term (exclusion restriction); used to obtain consistent estimates.
economics.2sls (two-stage least squares): An IV estimation procedure: first regress the endogenous variable on instruments and exogenous controls; then use fitted values in the second-stage regression.
economics.exclusion restriction: The identifying assumption that an instrument affects the outcome only through the endogenous variable of interest, not directly.
economics.difference-in-differences (did): A quasi-experimental method comparing changes in outcomes over time between a treatment group and a control group to identify causal effects.
economics.regression discontinuity (rdd): An identification strategy exploiting a known discontinuity in treatment assignment at a threshold of a running variable.
economics.fixed effects (fe): A panel data estimator that removes time-invariant unobserved heterogeneity by demeaning within each unit.
economics.random effects (re): A panel data estimator that treats the unit-level unobserved effects as random and uncorrelated with regressors; more efficient than FE if the assumption holds.
economics.hausman test: A specification test comparing FE and RE estimates; a significant difference indicates correlation between unobserved effects and regressors, favoring FE.
economics.late (local average treatment effect): The average treatment effect for compliers — units whose treatment status is changed by the instrument; the IV estimand under heterogeneous treatment effects.
economics.complier: An individual whose treatment status changes in the same direction as the instrument; IV identifies the treatment effect for compliers only.
economics.weak instruments: Instruments with low correlation with the endogenous variable, leading to biased IV estimates and invalid inference; diagnosed by first-stage F-statistic (rule of thumb: F > 10).
economics.parallel trends assumption: The identifying assumption for DiD: absent the treatment, the treated and control groups would have followed the same trend in outcomes.

mathematics.integer: A whole number, either positive, negative, or zero; an element of the set {..., -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, ...}.
mathematics.prime number: A natural number greater than 1 whose only positive divisors are 1 and itself.
mathematics.greatest common divisor (gcd): The largest positive integer that divides each of the given integers without remainder.
mathematics.least common multiple (lcm): The smallest positive integer that is divisible by each of the given integers.
mathematics.rational number: A number expressible as p/q where p and q are integers and q ≠ 0.
mathematics.absolute value: For a real number x, |x| = x if x ≥ 0 and |x| = -x if x < 0; the distance from x to 0 on the number line.
mathematics.proportion: An equation stating that two ratios are equal: a/b = c/d.
mathematics.exponent: In the expression a^n, the exponent n indicates the number of times the base a is multiplied by itself.
mathematics.polynomial: A finite sum of terms of the form a_n x^n where n is a non-negative integer and a_n are coefficients.
mathematics.degree of a polynomial: The highest power of the variable appearing with a nonzero coefficient.
mathematics.root (zero) of a polynomial: A value c such that p(c) = 0.
mathematics.discriminant: For ax² + bx + c, the discriminant is Δ = b² - 4ac; its sign determines the nature of the roots.
mathematics.complex number: A number of the form a + bi where a, b ∈ ℝ and i = √(-1).
mathematics.conjugate: The complex conjugate of a + bi is a - bi; used to rationalize denominators and compute moduli.
mathematics.multiplicity of a root: The number of times a root r appears as a factor; r has multiplicity k if (x - r)^k | p(x) but (x - r)^(k+1) does not.
mathematics.vieta's formulas: Relations between coefficients of a polynomial and symmetric functions of its roots; for x² + bx + c, the sum of roots is -b and product is c.
mathematics.congruent figures: Two geometric figures are congruent if one can be transformed into the other by a rigid motion (rotation, reflection, translation).
mathematics.similar figures: Two figures are similar if one is a dilation (scaling) of the other; corresponding angles are equal and corresponding sides are proportional.
mathematics.radian: The angle subtended at the center of a unit circle by an arc of length 1; π radians = 180 degrees.
mathematics.unit circle: The circle of radius 1 centered at the origin; defines sin θ and cos θ as the y- and x-coordinates of the point at angle θ.
mathematics.pythagorean triple: A set of three positive integers (a, b, c) satisfying a² + b² = c²; e.g., (3, 4, 5) and (5, 12, 13).
mathematics.inscribed angle: An angle formed by two chords sharing an endpoint on a circle; its measure is half the intercepted arc.
mathematics.tangent line: A line touching a circle at exactly one point, perpendicular to the radius at that point.
mathematics.dihedral angle: The angle between two half-planes sharing a common edge (line), measured in a plane perpendicular to that edge.
mathematics.function: A rule assigning to each element of a domain exactly one element of a codomain; formally, a subset of the Cartesian product where each input appears exactly once.
mathematics.injective (one-to-one): A function f is injective if f(a) = f(b) implies a = b; distinct inputs map to distinct outputs.
mathematics.surjective (onto): A function f: A → B is surjective if every element of B is f(a) for some a ∈ A.
mathematics.bijection: A function that is both injective and surjective; establishes a one-to-one correspondence between domain and codomain.
mathematics.inverse function: If f is bijective, its inverse f⁻¹ satisfies f⁻¹(f(x)) = x and f(f⁻¹(y)) = y for all x in the domain and y in the codomain.
mathematics.logarithm: log_b(x) = y means b^y = x; it is the inverse of exponentiation.
mathematics.asymptote: A line that a curve approaches arbitrarily closely but does not reach (horizontal, vertical, or oblique).
mathematics.arithmetic sequence: A sequence with a constant difference d between consecutive terms: a_n = a_1 + (n-1)d.
mathematics.geometric sequence: A sequence with a constant ratio r between consecutive terms: a_n = a_1 · r^(n-1).
mathematics.limit: lim_{x→a} f(x) = L means for every ε > 0 there exists δ > 0 such that 0 < |x - a| < δ implies |f(x) - L| < ε.
mathematics.continuity: f is continuous at a if lim_{x→a} f(x) = f(a); continuous on an interval if continuous at each point.
mathematics.derivative: f'(x) = lim_{h→0} (f(x+h) - f(x))/h; the instantaneous rate of change of f at x.
mathematics.riemann integral: ∫_a^b f(x) dx = lim_{||P||→0} Σ f(x_i*) Δx_i; the limit of Riemann sums over partitions P of [a, b].
mathematics.antiderivative: A function F such that F'(x) = f(x); also called an indefinite integral, written ∫ f(x) dx = F(x) + C.
mathematics.critical point: A point c in the domain of f where f'(c) = 0 or f'(c) does not exist.
mathematics.inflection point: A point where f''changes sign (concavity changes from up to down or vice versa).
mathematics.taylor series: The power series Σ_{n=0}^∞ f^(n)(a)/n! · (x-a)^n representing f near x = a, when f is infinitely differentiable.
mathematics.radius of convergence: For a power series Σ a_n x^n, the value R such that the series converges absolutely for |x| < R and diverges for |x| > R.
mathematics.partial derivative: ∂f/∂x is the derivative of f with respect to x holding all other variables fixed.
mathematics.gradient: ∇f = (∂f/∂x, ∂f/∂y, ∂f/∂z); a vector pointing in the direction of steepest ascent of f.
mathematics.directional derivative: D_u f(p) = ∇f(p) · u; the rate of change of f at p in the direction of unit vector u.
mathematics.jacobian: The matrix of all first-order partial derivatives of a vector-valued function; its determinant measures the local change of area/volume under a coordinate transformation.
mathematics.curl: curl F = ∇ × F; measures the infinitesimal rotation of a vector field at each point.
mathematics.divergence: div F = ∇ · F = ∂F₁/∂x + ∂F₂/∂y + ∂F₃/∂z; measures the net outflow of a vector field per unit volume.
mathematics.conservative vector field: A vector field F with curl F = 0 (equivalently, F = ∇f for some scalar potential f); line integrals are path-independent.
mathematics.saddle point: A critical point that is neither a local max nor a local min; the second derivative test gives a negative discriminant D = f_{xx}f_{yy} - f_{xy}².
mathematics.vector space: A set V with operations of addition and scalar multiplication satisfying 8 axioms (commutativity, associativity, distributivity, identities, inverses); elements are called vectors.
mathematics.linear independence: Vectors v₁,...,v_n are linearly independent if c₁v₁ + ... + c_n v_n = 0 implies all c_i = 0.
mathematics.basis: A linearly independent spanning set for a vector space; the number of elements is the dimension.
mathematics.rank: The dimension of the column space (or row space) of a matrix; equals the number of pivot columns in its row echelon form.
mathematics.nullspace (kernel): ker(A) = {x : Ax = 0}; a subspace of the domain whose dimension is the nullity.
mathematics.eigenvalue: A scalar λ such that Av = λv for some nonzero vector v (the corresponding eigenvector).
mathematics.characteristic polynomial: det(A - λI); its roots are the eigenvalues of A.
mathematics.orthogonal matrix: A square matrix Q with Q^T Q = I; its columns form an orthonormal basis; det Q = ±1.
mathematics.positive definite matrix: A symmetric matrix A such that x^T Ax > 0 for all nonzero x; equivalently, all eigenvalues are positive.
mathematics.singular matrix: A square matrix with determinant 0; equivalently, it has no inverse; equivalently, its columns are linearly dependent.
mathematics.ordinary differential equation (ode): An equation relating a function y(x) and its derivatives; order is the highest derivative present.
mathematics.separable ode: An ODE of the form dy/dx = f(x)g(y), solvable by separating variables: ∫ dy/g(y) = ∫ f(x) dx.
mathematics.integrating factor: A function μ(x) multiplied through a linear ODE dy/dx + P(x)y = Q(x) to make the left side an exact derivative; μ(x) = e^(∫P dx).
mathematics.characteristic equation: For ay'' + by' + cy = 0, the algebraic equation ar² + br + c = 0 whose roots determine the general solution.
mathematics.wronskian: W(y₁, y₂) = y₁y₂' - y₁'y₂; if W ≠ 0 on an interval, y₁ and y₂ are linearly independent solutions.
mathematics.laplace transform: L{f(t)} = F(s) = ∫_0^∞ e^(-st) f(t) dt; transforms a differential equation into an algebraic equation in s.
mathematics.equilibrium solution: A constant solution y = c where f(c) = 0; stable if nearby solutions approach it, unstable if they diverge.
mathematics.eigenfunction expansion: Representation of a function as a series of eigenfunctions of a differential operator (e.g., Fourier series in terms of sin and cos).
mathematics.sample space: The set Ω of all possible outcomes of a random experiment.
mathematics.event: A subset of the sample space; a measurable collection of outcomes to which a probability is assigned.
mathematics.conditional probability: P(A|B) = P(A ∩ B)/P(B) for P(B) > 0; the probability of A given that B has occurred.
mathematics.independence: Events A and B are independent if P(A ∩ B) = P(A)P(B); knowing B gives no information about A.
mathematics.random variable: A measurable function X: Ω → ℝ that assigns a numerical value to each outcome.
mathematics.expected value: E[X] = Σ x P(X=x) (discrete) or ∫ x f(x) dx (continuous); the probability-weighted average value.
mathematics.variance: Var(X) = E[(X - μ)²] = E[X²] - (E[X])²; measures spread around the mean.
mathematics.moment generating function: M_X(t) = E[e^{tX}]; its k-th derivative at t=0 equals the k-th moment E[X^k].
mathematics.cumulative distribution function: F(x) = P(X ≤ x); a right-continuous, non-decreasing function from ℝ to [0,1].
mathematics.population vs. sample: A population is the entire set of subjects; a sample is a subset drawn from it. Statistics from samples estimate population parameters.
mathematics.standard error: The standard deviation of the sampling distribution of a statistic; for the sample mean, SE = σ/√n.
mathematics.confidence interval: An interval [L, U] constructed from data such that it contains the true parameter with a stated probability (confidence level) in repeated sampling.
mathematics.null hypothesis (h₀): The default claim being tested; typically a statement of no effect or no difference.
mathematics.p-value: The probability of obtaining a test statistic at least as extreme as observed, assuming H₀ is true; small p-values provide evidence against H₀.
mathematics.type i error (α): Rejecting H₀ when it is true; probability is the significance level α.
mathematics.type ii error (β): Failing to reject H₀ when it is false; power = 1 - β.
mathematics.correlation coefficient (r): r = Cov(X,Y)/(σ_X σ_Y); ranges from -1 to 1; measures the linear association between two variables.
mathematics.regression coefficient: In ŷ = a + bx, the slope b = r · (s_y/s_x) = Σ(x_i - x̄)(y_i - ȳ) / Σ(x_i - x̄)².
mathematics.group: A set G with a binary operation · satisfying: (i) closure, (ii) associativity, (iii) existence of identity e with ge = eg = g, (iv) existence of inverses g⁻¹ with gg⁻¹ = e.
mathematics.abelian group: A group in which the operation is commutative: ab = ba for all a, b.
mathematics.order of a group: |G|, the cardinality of the set G.
mathematics.order of an element: The smallest positive integer n such that g^n = e; if no such n exists, the element has infinite order.
mathematics.subgroup: A subset H ⊆ G closed under the group operation and inverses, forming a group in its own right. Equivalently: H ≠ ∅ and a,b ∈ H ⟹ ab⁻¹ ∈ H.
mathematics.normal subgroup: A subgroup N ⊴ G such that gNg⁻¹ = N for all g ∈ G; equivalently, left and right cosets coincide.
mathematics.coset: A left coset of H in G is gH = {gh : h ∈ H}; the cosets partition G into equal-sized subsets.
mathematics.quotient group: G/N where N ⊴ G; the set of cosets {gN} with multiplication (aN)(bN) = (ab)N.
mathematics.group homomorphism: A function φ: G → H satisfying φ(ab) = φ(a)φ(b) for all a, b ∈ G.
mathematics.kernel: ker φ = {g ∈ G : φ(g) = e_H}; always a normal subgroup of G.
mathematics.simple group: A nontrivial group with no proper nontrivial normal subgroups.
mathematics.p-group: A group whose order is a power of a prime p; every nontrivial p-group has a nontrivial center.
mathematics.ring: A set R with addition and multiplication satisfying: (R,+) is an abelian group, multiplication is associative, and distributive laws hold.
mathematics.commutative ring: A ring in which multiplication is commutative: ab = ba for all a, b.
mathematics.unity (multiplicative identity): An element 1 ∈ R such that 1·a = a·1 = a for all a; a ring with unity is called a unital ring.
mathematics.ideal: A subset I ⊆ R closed under addition and satisfying rI ⊆ I and Ir ⊆ I for all r ∈ R; the kernel of any ring homomorphism is an ideal.
mathematics.prime ideal: An ideal P ≠ R such that ab ∈ P implies a ∈ P or b ∈ P; R/P is an integral domain iff P is prime.
mathematics.maximal ideal: An ideal M ≠ R with no ideal strictly between M and R; R/M is a field iff M is maximal.
mathematics.integral domain: A commutative unital ring with no zero divisors; ab = 0 implies a = 0 or b = 0.
mathematics.field: A commutative ring in which every nonzero element has a multiplicative inverse; equivalently, a commutative division ring.
mathematics.ufd (unique factorization domain): An integral domain in which every nonzero non-unit element factors uniquely into irreducibles (up to order and units).
mathematics.pid (principal ideal domain): An integral domain in which every ideal is principal, i.e., generated by a single element.
mathematics.algebraic element: An element α over a field F is algebraic if it satisfies some nonzero polynomial with coefficients in F; otherwise it is transcendental.
mathematics.degree of field extension: [E:F] = dim_F E; the dimension of E as a vector space over F.
mathematics.completeness: A metric space is complete if every Cauchy sequence converges; the reals are the unique (up to isomorphism) complete ordered field.
mathematics.open set: A set U in a metric space such that every point has an open ball entirely within U; equivalently, U = int(U).
mathematics.compact set: A set K where every open cover has a finite subcover; in ℝⁿ, equivalently, closed and bounded (Heine-Borel).
mathematics.uniform continuity: f is uniformly continuous on E if for all ε > 0 there exists δ > 0 such that d(x,y) < δ implies |f(x)-f(y)| < ε, with δ independent of the choice of x.
mathematics.uniform convergence: f_n → f uniformly on E if sup_{x∈E} |f_n(x) - f(x)| → 0; it preserves continuity, and allows interchange of limit and integral.
mathematics.σ-algebra: A collection Σ of subsets of a set X closed under complements and countable unions; provides the domain for a measure.
mathematics.measure: A countably additive function μ: Σ → [0,∞] on a σ-algebra with μ(∅) = 0.
mathematics.lebesgue measurable: A set E ⊆ ℝ is Lebesgue measurable if for all ε > 0 there is an open set U ⊇ E with m(U\ E) < ε (outer-regularity characterization).
mathematics.almost everywhere (a.e.): A property holds almost everywhere if the set of points where it fails has measure zero.
mathematics.l^p space: L^p(μ) = {f measurable : ∫|f|^p dμ < ∞} with norm ‖f‖_p = (∫|f|^p dμ)^(1/p); a Banach space for 1 ≤ p ≤ ∞.
mathematics.holomorphic function: A function f: U → ℂ (U open in ℂ) that is complex-differentiable at every point of U; equivalently, satisfies the Cauchy-Riemann equations with continuous partials.
mathematics.analytic function: A function representable by a convergent power series in a neighborhood of each point; for complex functions, analyticity and holomorphicity are equivalent.
mathematics.cauchy-riemann equations: For f = u + iv: ∂u/∂x = ∂v/∂y and ∂u/∂y = -∂v/∂x; necessary and (with continuous partials) sufficient for holomorphicity.
mathematics.contour integral: ∫_γ f(z) dz = ∫_a^b f(γ(t))γ'(t) dt for a parameterized curve γ: [a,b] → ℂ.
mathematics.isolated singularity: A point z₀ where f is not defined or not holomorphic, but f is holomorphic in some punctured disk 0 < |z - z₀| < r.
mathematics.residue: Res(f, z₀) = a₋₁, the coefficient of (z - z₀)⁻¹ in the Laurent expansion of f at z₀; for a simple pole: Res(f, z₀) = lim_{z→z₀} (z-z₀)f(z).
mathematics.möbius transformation: A map z ↦ (az+b)/(cz+d) with ad - bc ≠ 0; a bijection of the Riemann sphere ℂ∪{∞} that maps circles and lines to circles and lines.
mathematics.entire function: A function holomorphic on all of ℂ (no singularities in the finite plane); e.g., e^z, sin z, polynomials.
mathematics.meromorphic function: A function holomorphic on a domain except at isolated poles; locally a ratio of holomorphic functions.
mathematics.congruence: a ≡ b (mod n) means n divides (a - b); this is an equivalence relation on ℤ compatible with + and ×.
mathematics.euler's totient function φ(n): The count of integers in {1,...,n} coprime to n; φ(n) = n ∏_{p|n} (1 - 1/p).
mathematics.primitive root (mod n): An integer g whose powers g¹, g², ..., g^{φ(n)} generate all units mod n; exists iff n = 1, 2, 4, p^k, or 2p^k for odd prime p.
mathematics.quadratic residue: An integer a coprime to p is a quadratic residue mod p if a ≡ x² (mod p) has a solution; otherwise a nonresidue.
mathematics.legendre symbol: (a/p) = 1 if a is a QR mod p, -1 if nonresidue, 0 if p|a; computable via Euler's criterion (a/p) ≡ a^((p-1)/2) (mod p).
mathematics.arithmetic function: A function f: ℕ → ℂ; it is multiplicative if gcd(m,n)=1 implies f(mn)=f(m)f(n).
mathematics.möbius function μ(n): μ(1)=1; μ(n)=(-1)^k if n is a product of k distinct primes; μ(n)=0 if n has a squared prime factor.
mathematics.gaussian integer: A complex number a + bi with a, b ∈ ℤ; forms a UFD Z[i] in which the Euclidean algorithm applies with norm N(a+bi) = a²+b².
mathematics.partial order: A relation ≤ on a set that is reflexive (a≤a), antisymmetric (a≤b and b≤a implies a=b), and transitive (a≤b and b≤c implies a≤c).
mathematics.equivalence relation: A relation that is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive; partitions the set into equivalence classes.
mathematics.graph: A pair G = (V, E) of a vertex set V and an edge set E ⊆ V×V; may be directed or undirected, simple or multi.
mathematics.tree: A connected acyclic graph; a tree on n vertices has exactly n-1 edges.
mathematics.planar graph: A graph that can be drawn in the plane without edge crossings.
mathematics.chromatic number χ(g): The minimum number of colors needed to color the vertices of G so that no two adjacent vertices share a color.
mathematics.generating function: A formal power series f(x) = Σ a_n x^n encoding the sequence (a_n); used to solve recurrences and count combinatorial structures.
mathematics.derangement: A permutation with no fixed points; the number of derangements D_n = n! Σ_{k=0}^{n} (-1)^k/k! ≈ n!/e.
mathematics.catalan number: C_n = (1/(n+1))C(2n,n); counts many combinatorial structures including valid parenthesizations, binary trees, and Dyck paths.
mathematics.topological space: A set X with a topology τ (a collection of open sets) satisfying: ∅ and X are open, arbitrary unions of open sets are open, and finite intersections of open sets are open.
mathematics.homeomorphism: A bijection f: X → Y such that both f and f⁻¹ are continuous; X and Y are topologically identical.
mathematics.topological invariant: A property preserved by homeomorphism, e.g., compactness, connectedness, Betti numbers.
mathematics.connected space: A topological space that cannot be partitioned into two disjoint nonempty open sets.
mathematics.compact space: A topological space where every open cover has a finite subcover.
mathematics.hausdorff space (t₂): A space where any two distinct points have disjoint open neighborhoods; metric spaces are Hausdorff.
mathematics.homotopy: A continuous deformation of one continuous map into another; f, g: X→Y are homotopic if there exists H: X×[0,1]→Y with H(x,0)=f(x) and H(x,1)=g(x).
mathematics.fundamental group π₁(x, x₀): The group of homotopy classes of loops based at x₀ in X, with concatenation as the group operation; measures 1-dimensional 'holes'.
mathematics.simply connected: A path-connected space with trivial fundamental group (π₁ = {e}); every loop is contractible to a point.
mathematics.covering space: A space X̃ with a surjection p: X̃ → X such that every point of X has an open neighborhood U whose preimage is a disjoint union of copies of U.

physics.inertia: The tendency of an object to resist changes in its state of motion, quantified by mass.
physics.momentum: The product of an object's mass and velocity, p = mv; a conserved quantity in isolated systems.
physics.impulse: The change in momentum of an object, equal to the net force integrated over the time interval it acts.
physics.torque: The rotational analog of force, defined as the cross product of the position vector and the applied force: τ = r × F.
physics.moment of inertia: The rotational analog of mass; measures a body's resistance to angular acceleration: I = sum(m_i * r_i^2).
physics.angular momentum: The rotational analog of linear momentum: L = I * ω = r × p; conserved when net torque is zero.
physics.center of mass: The mass-weighted average position of all particles in a system; the system behaves as if all its mass is concentrated there under external forces.
physics.conservative force: A force whose work done on a particle moving between two points is independent of the path taken; derivable from a potential energy function.
physics.simple harmonic motion: Oscillatory motion governed by a restoring force proportional to displacement: F = -kx, producing sinusoidal position as a function of time.
physics.escape velocity: The minimum speed required for an object to escape a gravitational field without further propulsion: v_esc = sqrt(2GM/r).
physics.bernoulli's principle: Along a streamline in steady, incompressible, inviscid flow, the sum of pressure, kinetic energy density, and gravitational potential energy density is constant.
physics.reynolds number: Re = ρvL/μ; the ratio of inertial to viscous forces; low Re → laminar flow; high Re → turbulent flow; transition typically Re ~ 10^3–10^4.
physics.generalized coordinates: Any set of independent parameters q_i that uniquely specifies the configuration of a system, not necessarily Cartesian.
physics.holonomic constraint: A constraint expressible as an algebraic relation among the generalized coordinates (and possibly time): f(q_1,...,q_n,t) = 0.
physics.lagrangian: The function L = T - V (kinetic minus potential energy) whose integral over time, the action, is stationary along the physical trajectory.
physics.action: The functional S[q] = integral(L(q, dq/dt, t) dt) whose stationary paths (δS = 0) yield the equations of motion.
physics.euler-lagrange equation: The differential equation d/dt(∂L/∂q̇_i) - ∂L/∂q_i = 0 that a path must satisfy to render the action stationary.
physics.cyclic coordinate: A generalized coordinate q_i that does not appear explicitly in L (only q̇_i does), implying its conjugate momentum p_i = ∂L/∂q̇_i is conserved.
physics.conjugate momentum: The quantity p_i = ∂L/∂q̇_i canonically paired with the generalized coordinate q_i; reduces to ordinary momentum in Cartesian coordinates.
physics.hamiltonian: H(q,p,t) = sum_i(p_i * q̇_i) - L, the Legendre transform of the Lagrangian; equals the total energy when L has no explicit time dependence and constraints are scleronomic.
physics.legendre transformation: A mathematical transform that switches between functions of complementary variables; converts L(q,q̇) to H(q,p).
physics.canonical transformation: A change of phase-space variables (q,p) → (Q,P) that preserves the form of Hamilton's equations, i.e., preserves the Poisson bracket structure.
physics.poisson bracket: The bilinear operation {f,g} = sum_i(∂f/∂q_i * ∂g/∂p_i - ∂f/∂p_i * ∂g/∂q_i) measuring how rapidly one observable changes along the flow generated by another.
physics.action-angle variables: Canonical variables (J, θ) where J is the action integral (adiabatic invariant) and θ advances uniformly in time; natural for integrable systems.
physics.adiabatic invariant: A quantity J = (1/2π) * ∮ p dq that is approximately conserved when system parameters change slowly compared to the oscillation period.
physics.electric field: A vector field E(r) such that the force on a test charge q is F = qE; created by charges and time-varying magnetic fields.
physics.electric potential: A scalar field V(r) such that E = -∇V; the work done per unit charge moving from infinity to the point r.
physics.gauss's law: The total electric flux through a closed surface equals the enclosed charge divided by ε_0: ∮ E · dA = Q_enc/ε_0.
physics.faraday's law: A time-varying magnetic flux through a loop induces an EMF equal to minus the rate of change of flux: EMF = -dΦ_B/dt.
physics.ampère-maxwell law: The curl of B equals μ_0 times the sum of free current density and the displacement current density ε_0 ∂E/∂t.
physics.displacement current: Maxwell's addition to Ampère's law: J_d = ε_0 ∂E/∂t; ensures charge conservation and predicts electromagnetic wave propagation in vacuum.
physics.poynting vector: S = (1/μ_0) * E × B; represents the directional energy flux density of an electromagnetic field (power per unit area).
physics.electromagnetic wave: A self-sustaining oscillation of coupled E and B fields propagating at speed c = 1/sqrt(ε_0 μ_0) in vacuum; E and B are perpendicular to each other and to the direction of propagation.
physics.magnetic vector potential: A vector field A such that B = ∇ × A; used with scalar potential φ to encode the full EM field in Lagrangian/quantum treatments.
physics.lenz's law: The induced EMF always acts to oppose the change in magnetic flux that produced it; a consequence of energy conservation.
physics.boundary conditions (em): At an interface: tangential E and normal B are continuous; tangential H and normal D jump by the surface free current/charge densities.
physics.skin depth: The characteristic depth δ = sqrt(2/(μ σ ω)) at which electromagnetic fields penetrate a conductor before attenuating by 1/e.
physics.wave function: A complex-valued function ψ(r,t) whose modulus squared |ψ|^2 gives the probability density of finding the particle at position r at time t.
physics.born rule: The probability of measuring observable A and obtaining eigenvalue a_n is |<a_n|ψ>|^2; postulate connecting the mathematical formalism to experimental outcomes.
physics.hilbert space: The complete complex inner-product space of normalizable quantum states; observables are Hermitian operators on this space.
physics.hermitian operator: An operator Â satisfying Â† = Â; its eigenvalues are real and eigenvectors form a complete orthonormal basis, making it suitable to represent a physical observable.
physics.commutator: For operators Â and B̂, [Â,B̂] = ÂB̂ - B̂Â; non-zero commutator means the observables cannot be simultaneously specified with arbitrary precision.
physics.heisenberg uncertainty principle: For conjugate variables position and momentum: σ_x * σ_p ≥ ℏ/2; a consequence of the non-commutativity [x̂, p̂] = iℏ.
physics.stationary state: An eigenstate of the Hamiltonian Ĥ|E_n> = E_n|E_n>; its probability density is time-independent.
physics.ladder operators: Operators â and â† defined for the harmonic oscillator by â = (x̂ + ip̂/mω)/sqrt(2mωℏ) and its conjugate; â†|n> = sqrt(n+1)|n+1>, â|n> = sqrt(n)|n-1>.
physics.spin: An intrinsic angular momentum of a quantum particle with no classical analogue; spin-1/2 particles have eigenvalues ±ℏ/2 and are described by Pauli matrices.
physics.pauli matrices: Three 2×2 Hermitian matrices σ_x, σ_y, σ_z representing the spin-1/2 operators (up to ℏ/2); satisfy σ_i σ_j = δ_ij I + i ε_ijk σ_k.
physics.time evolution operator: The unitary operator Û(t,t_0) = exp(-iĤ(t-t_0)/ℏ) for time-independent Ĥ; propagates states forward: |ψ(t)> = Û(t,t_0)|ψ(t_0)>.
physics.fermi's golden rule: The transition rate from state |i> to states |f> under perturbation H' is Γ = (2π/ℏ)|<f|H'|i>|^2 * ρ(E_f); valid to first-order time-dependent perturbation theory.
physics.variational principle: For any normalized trial state |φ>, <φ|Ĥ|φ> ≥ E_0 (the true ground-state energy); enables upper-bound estimates of E_0.
physics.wkb approximation: A semiclassical approximation valid when the de Broglie wavelength changes slowly; the wave function in a slowly varying potential is ψ ~ exp(±i ∫ p(x) dx/ℏ)/sqrt(p(x)).
physics.scattering cross section: The effective area σ that characterizes the probability of a scattering event; differential cross section dσ/dΩ = |f(θ,φ)|^2 where f is the scattering amplitude.
physics.entropy: A state function S = k_B * ln(Ω) (Boltzmann) or S = -k_B * sum(p_i * ln p_i) (Gibbs); quantifies the number of accessible microstates and the arrow of time.
physics.partition function: Z = sum_i exp(-β E_i) (canonical), where β = 1/(k_B T); the generating function for all thermodynamic quantities.
physics.canonical ensemble: Statistical description of a system in thermal contact with a heat reservoir at temperature T; probability of state i is p_i = exp(-β E_i)/Z.
physics.grand canonical ensemble: Statistical description of a system exchanging both energy and particles with a reservoir; characterized by T and chemical potential μ.
physics.grand partition function: Z_G = sum_{N,i} exp(-β(E_{N,i} - μN)) = sum_N z^N * Z_N, where z = exp(βμ) is the fugacity.
physics.chemical potential: μ = (∂F/∂N)_{T,V} = (∂G/∂N)_{T,P}; the free energy cost of adding one particle to the system at constant T and V (or P).
physics.equipartition theorem: In classical statistical mechanics, each quadratic degree of freedom in the Hamiltonian contributes (1/2)k_B T to the mean energy.
physics.fermi-dirac distribution: The mean occupation number of a single-particle state with energy ε for identical fermions: f_FD(ε) = 1/(exp(β(ε-μ))+1).
physics.bose-einstein distribution: The mean occupation number of a single-particle state with energy ε for identical bosons: f_BE(ε) = 1/(exp(β(ε-μ))-1).
physics.fermi energy: The chemical potential of an ideal Fermi gas at T = 0; the energy of the highest occupied state in the ground state.
physics.bose-einstein condensation: The macroscopic occupation of the ground single-particle state below a critical temperature T_c in a gas of non-interacting bosons.
physics.free energy (helmholtz): F = U - TS; the thermodynamic potential minimized at equilibrium for a system at constant T and V; F = -k_B T * ln Z.
physics.gibbs free energy: G = U + PV - TS = H - TS; the potential minimized at equilibrium for constant T and P; determines spontaneity of chemical/phase transitions.
physics.fluctuation-dissipation theorem: Relates spontaneous fluctuations of a system in equilibrium to its linear response (dissipation) to an external perturbation; e.g., Johnson-Nyquist noise in resistors.
physics.spacetime interval: The Lorentz-invariant quantity s^2 = -(c*Δt)^2 + Δx^2 + Δy^2 + Δz^2 (or its negative, depending on sign convention); the same in all inertial frames.
physics.lorentz factor: γ = 1/sqrt(1 - v^2/c^2); the factor by which time dilates and length contracts for objects moving at speed v.
physics.proper time: The time elapsed on a clock moving with the object, related to coordinate time by dτ = dt/γ; the Lorentz-invariant measure of elapsed time along a worldline.
physics.proper length: The length of an object measured in its rest frame; an observer in relative motion measures a shorter length L = L_0/γ (length contraction).
physics.4-vector: A set of four quantities (A^0, A^1, A^2, A^3) that transforms under Lorentz transformations like (ct, x, y, z); its norm A^μ A_μ is Lorentz invariant.
physics.energy-momentum 4-vector: p^μ = (E/c, p_x, p_y, p_z); its invariant norm gives p^μ p_μ = -(mc)^2, encoding the mass-energy-momentum relation.
physics.rest energy: The energy of an object at rest: E_0 = m*c^2; represents the conversion equivalence between mass and energy.
physics.rapidity: The parameter φ = tanh^{-1}(v/c) = (1/2)ln((1+β)/(1-β)); Lorentz boosts add rapidities, making it convenient for composing velocities.
physics.time dilation: A moving clock ticks slower: Δt = γ * Δτ; an observer measures a longer time between events on a moving clock than the proper time.
physics.relativistic velocity addition: When a particle moves with velocity u' in frame S' and S' moves with v relative to S, the velocity in S is u = (u'+v)/(1 + u'v/c^2).
physics.equivalence principle: Locally, a uniformly accelerating frame is indistinguishable from a gravitational field (Einstein's version); gravity is not a force but geometry.
physics.metric tensor: The symmetric tensor g_{μν}(x) encoding the geometry of spacetime; defines the line element ds^2 = g_{μν} dx^μ dx^ν and hence distances, angles, and time elapsed.
physics.geodesic: The generalization of a straight line to curved spacetime; the path of a free-falling particle (no non-gravitational forces) that extremizes proper time.
physics.christoffel symbol: Γ^λ_{μν} = (1/2)g^{λσ}(∂_μ g_{νσ} + ∂_ν g_{μσ} - ∂_σ g_{μν}); the connection coefficients encoding parallel transport on the curved manifold.
physics.riemann curvature tensor: R^λ_{ρμν} = ∂_μ Γ^λ_{νρ} - ∂_ν Γ^λ_{μρ} + Γ^λ_{μσ}Γ^σ_{νρ} - Γ^λ_{νσ}Γ^σ_{μρ}; measures the failure of parallel transport around a closed loop.
physics.ricci tensor: R_{μν} = R^λ_{μλν}; the contraction of the Riemann tensor that appears in the Einstein field equations.
physics.einstein tensor: G_{μν} = R_{μν} - (1/2)g_{μν}R; divergence-free (∇^μ G_{μν} = 0), consistent with energy-momentum conservation.
physics.stress-energy tensor: T_{μν} encodes the density and flux of energy and momentum; T^{00} = energy density, T^{0i} = momentum density; the source of gravity in GR.
physics.schwarzschild radius: r_s = 2GM/c^2; the radius at which the Schwarzschild metric becomes singular; the event horizon of a non-rotating black hole.
physics.event horizon: A null hypersurface beyond which events cannot causally influence the exterior; in the Schwarzschild metric, located at r = r_s = 2GM/c^2.
physics.gravitational wave: A propagating perturbation h_{μν} = g_{μν} - η_{μν} of spacetime geometry, sourced by time-varying quadrupole mass distributions; travels at c and carries energy.
physics.cosmological constant: Λ, Einstein's term added to the field equations representing a constant energy density of vacuum; currently interpreted as dark energy driving accelerated expansion.
physics.bravais lattice: A periodic array of points R = n_1 a_1 + n_2 a_2 + n_3 a_3 (n_i integers) that looks identical from every lattice point; there are 14 distinct Bravais lattices in 3D.
physics.reciprocal lattice: The lattice of wave vectors G satisfying exp(iG·R) = 1 for all Bravais lattice vectors R; the natural space for diffraction and Bloch states.
physics.brillouin zone: The Wigner-Seitz cell of the reciprocal lattice; the set of k-vectors closest to the origin; the irreducible domain for Bloch band structure.
physics.bloch's theorem: The eigenstates of a Hamiltonian with lattice periodicity have the form ψ_{nk}(r) = u_{nk}(r)exp(ik·r) where u has the periodicity of the lattice.
physics.band gap: An energy range in which no electronic states exist in a crystalline solid; arises from Bragg scattering at Brillouin zone boundaries; determines semiconductor/insulator character.
physics.effective mass: m* = ℏ^2 / (d^2E/dk^2); the curvature of the band dispersion; can be negative near band maxima (holes behave as positive-mass positive-charge carriers).
physics.phonon: A quantum of lattice vibration; a bosonic collective excitation with dispersion ω(k) governed by interatomic force constants; carries heat but not charge.
physics.debye model: Approximates the phonon density of states as ∝ ω^2 up to a cutoff ω_D; correctly gives C_V ~ T^3 at low temperature (Debye T^3 law).
physics.fermi surface: The constant-energy surface in k-space at the Fermi energy; determines low-temperature electronic properties; topological changes drive Lifshitz transitions.
physics.cooper pair: A bound pair of electrons with opposite momenta and spins, stabilized by an attractive phonon-mediated interaction; the fundamental unit of the BCS superconducting condensate.
physics.meissner effect: The expulsion of magnetic flux from a superconductor below T_c; not merely perfect conductivity but active flux expulsion, confirming the thermodynamic nature of the superconducting state.
physics.order parameter: A quantity (e.g., magnetization M, gap Δ in BCS, superfluid density) that is zero in the disordered phase and non-zero in the ordered phase; its symmetry and spatial variation classify the transition.
physics.standard model: The gauge field theory SU(3)_C × SU(2)_L × U(1)_Y describing strong, weak, and electromagnetic interactions; all matter fields are quarks and leptons (3 generations each).
physics.quark: A spin-1/2 fermion carrying color charge (r,g,b) and fractional electric charge; confined in hadrons; 6 flavors: u,d,s,c,b,t.
physics.lepton: A spin-1/2 fermion without color charge; 6 types: e, μ, τ and their neutrinos ν_e, ν_μ, ν_τ; each carries lepton-family number.
physics.gauge boson: A spin-1 force-carrying particle arising from local gauge symmetry: photon (U(1)), W^±/Z (SU(2)), gluons g (SU(3)); masses generated by Higgs mechanism except photon and gluon.
physics.feynman diagram: A pictorial representation of terms in a perturbative expansion of scattering amplitudes; vertices and propagators encode interaction strengths and particle propagation.
physics.propagator: The momentum-space Green's function for a quantum field; for a scalar: 1/(p^2 - m^2 + iε); encodes virtual particle exchange between vertices.
physics.running coupling: The effective coupling constant α(μ) that depends on the energy scale μ due to quantum loop corrections (renormalization group); α_s decreases at high energy (asymptotic freedom).
physics.spontaneous symmetry breaking: A system whose equations are symmetric but whose ground state is not; in the Higgs mechanism, the electroweak SU(2)_L×U(1)_Y symmetry breaks to U(1)_EM, generating W/Z masses.
physics.higgs boson: The scalar boson (spin-0, m ≈ 125 GeV) arising as the excitation of the Higgs field after spontaneous electroweak symmetry breaking; observed at LHC in 2012.
physics.color confinement: The property of QCD whereby free color-charged particles (quarks, gluons) are never observed; the strong force potential grows with distance, confining quarks inside hadrons.
physics.asymptotic freedom: The decrease of the QCD coupling α_s at high energies (short distances), allowing perturbative calculations of hard processes; established by Gross, Politzer, and Wilczek (2004 Nobel Prize).
physics.cp violation: The violation of combined charge-conjugation (C) and parity (P) symmetry; observed in kaon and B-meson systems; necessary (Sakharov conditions) for matter-antimatter asymmetry.
physics.hubble's law: Galaxies recede with velocity proportional to distance: v = H_0 * d, where H_0 ≈ 70 km/s/Mpc is the Hubble constant; evidence for an expanding universe.
physics.hubble parameter: H(t) = (da/dt)/a where a(t) is the cosmic scale factor; H_0 is its present value; governs the rate of cosmic expansion.
physics.scale factor: a(t), a dimensionless function normalised to a(t_0)=1 today; physical distances scale as d_phys = a(t)*d_comoving; its evolution is governed by the Friedmann equations.
physics.chandrasekhar limit: The maximum mass (~1.4 M_sun) of a white dwarf supported by electron degeneracy pressure; exceeded mass collapses to a neutron star or black hole.
physics.cosmic microwave background: The thermal relic radiation from recombination (~380,000 yr after Big Bang) at z ≈ 1100; a near-perfect blackbody at T_0 = 2.725 K with anisotropies ΔT/T ~ 10^{-5} encoding structure formation.
physics.dark matter: Non-luminous matter constituting ~27% of the cosmic energy budget; inferred from galactic rotation curves, gravitational lensing, and CMB power spectra; particle nature unknown.
physics.dark energy: An energy component (~68% of cosmic budget) causing accelerated expansion; consistent with a cosmological constant Λ with equation of state w = P/ρ = -1.
physics.redshift: The fractional wavelength shift z = (λ_obs - λ_emit)/λ_emit; for cosmological sources, 1+z = 1/a(t_emit), linking observed wavelength to the scale factor at emission.
physics.luminosity distance: d_L = (1+z)*d_comoving; the distance inferred from the observed flux of a standard candle: F = L/(4π d_L^2).
physics.equation of state (cosmology): w = P/ρ characterizing a cosmic fluid: w=0 (matter/dust), w=1/3 (radiation), w=-1 (cosmological constant/dark energy); determines how ρ scales with a.
physics.inflation: A period of accelerated exponential expansion in the early universe (t ~ 10^{-36} s) driven by a slow-rolling inflaton field; solves the horizon, flatness, and monopole problems and seeds CMB fluctuations.
physics.constructive interference: Superposition of two waves in phase (path difference = nλ), producing amplitude addition and maximum intensity.
physics.destructive interference: Superposition of two waves in antiphase (path difference = (n+1/2)λ), producing amplitude cancellation and minimum intensity.
physics.diffraction: The bending and spreading of waves around obstacles or through apertures; governed by Huygens' principle and characterized by Fraunhofer or Fresnel regimes.
physics.snell's law: At an interface between two media, n_1 sin θ_1 = n_2 sin θ_2, relating the angles of incidence and refraction to the indices of refraction.
physics.index of refraction: n = c/v_phase; the ratio of the speed of light in vacuum to its phase velocity in the medium; determines refraction angles and optical path length.
physics.total internal reflection: When light in a denser medium strikes an interface at angle greater than θ_c = sin^{-1}(n_2/n_1), it reflects completely back; the basis for optical fibers.
physics.brewster's angle: The angle θ_B = tan^{-1}(n_2/n_1) at which reflected light is completely s-polarized; at this angle the reflected and transmitted beams are perpendicular.
physics.coherence length: The path length difference over which two beams from the same source maintain a fixed phase relationship; inversely proportional to the source's spectral bandwidth.
physics.group velocity: v_g = dω/dk; the velocity at which the envelope of a wave packet and hence energy/information propagates; differs from phase velocity v_φ = ω/k in dispersive media.
physics.doppler effect: The shift in observed frequency when source and observer are in relative motion: f_obs = f_src*(v_wave ± v_obs)/(v_wave ∓ v_src).
physics.binding energy: The energy required to disassemble a nucleus into its constituent protons and neutrons: B = (Z*m_p + N*m_n - M_nucleus)*c^2.
physics.mass defect: The difference between the total mass of individual nucleons and the actual nuclear mass: Δm = Z*m_p + N*m_n - M; equals B/c^2.
physics.radioactive decay law: The number of undecayed nuclei decreases exponentially: N(t) = N_0 exp(-λt), where λ is the decay constant; half-life t_{1/2} = ln2/λ.
physics.alpha decay: Emission of a ^4He nucleus; governed by quantum tunneling through the Coulomb barrier; described by the Geiger-Nuttall law.
physics.beta decay (β^-): Conversion of a neutron to a proton with emission of an electron and anti-electron-neutrino: n → p + e^- + ν̄_e; mediated by the weak force.
physics.q-value: Q = (m_reactants - m_products)*c^2; the energy released (Q > 0, exothermic) or absorbed (Q < 0, endothermic) in a nuclear reaction.
physics.fine structure: Small splitting of atomic energy levels due to spin-orbit coupling (L·S interaction), relativistic corrections, and the Darwin term; of order α^2 E_n.
physics.selection rules (electric dipole): Transitions are allowed only if Δl = ±1, Δm_l = 0, ±1, Δm_s = 0; forbidden otherwise (no photon angular momentum budget).
physics.zeeman effect: Splitting of atomic spectral lines in an external magnetic field; normal Zeeman: triplet (for S=0 states); anomalous Zeeman involves spin.
physics.hyperfine structure: Very small splitting of atomic levels due to the interaction between the nuclear magnetic moment and the electronic magnetic field at the nucleus; source of the 21 cm hydrogen line.
physics.thermal equilibrium: Two systems are in thermal equilibrium if they share the same temperature and no net heat flows between them; basis of the zeroth law.
physics.internal energy: The total microscopic kinetic and potential energy of a system's constituent particles; a state function whose change equals heat added minus work done by the system.
physics.adiabatic process: A thermodynamic process in which no heat is exchanged with the surroundings (Q = 0); for an ideal gas: PV^γ = constant where γ = C_P/C_V.
physics.heat capacity: The energy required to raise the temperature of a substance by one degree: C = δQ/dT; at constant volume C_V = (∂U/∂T)_V; at constant pressure C_P = (∂H/∂T)_P.
physics.carnot efficiency: The maximum efficiency of any heat engine operating between reservoirs at T_H and T_C: η_Carnot = 1 - T_C/T_H; achieved only by a reversible engine.
physics.entropy (classical): A state function defined by dS = δQ_rev/T for reversible processes; always increases in irreversible processes in an isolated system (second law).
physics.equation of state: A relation among the thermodynamic state variables (e.g., P, V, T, N) of a system; the ideal gas law PV = nRT is the simplest example.
physics.specific heat ratio: γ = C_P/C_V; for ideal monatomic gas γ = 5/3; diatomic γ = 7/5; determines the slope of adiabats and the speed of sound.
physics.gradient: ∇f = (∂f/∂x, ∂f/∂y, ∂f/∂z); a vector pointing in the direction of maximum rate of increase of scalar field f.
physics.divergence: ∇·F = ∂F_x/∂x + ∂F_y/∂y + ∂F_z/∂z; measures the outward flux per unit volume of a vector field; appears in Gauss's law.
physics.curl: ∇×F; a vector field whose magnitude and direction encode the circulation per unit area of F; appears in Faraday's and Ampère's laws.
physics.dirac delta function: A generalized function δ(x-a) = 0 for x≠a and ∫ δ(x-a)f(x)dx = f(a); used to represent point sources and initial conditions.
physics.green's function: G(x,x') satisfying L G(x,x') = δ(x-x') for linear operator L; enables solutions to inhomogeneous equations as y(x) = ∫ G(x,x') f(x') dx'.
physics.legendre polynomials: P_l(cos θ) are solutions to Legendre's equation arising in azimuthally symmetric problems (e.g., multipole expansion, hydrogen atom); orthogonal on [-1,1].
physics.bessel functions: J_n(x) and Y_n(x) are solutions to Bessel's equation, arising in cylindrical symmetry problems; J_n is regular at the origin, Y_n diverges there.
physics.fourier transform: f̃(k) = ∫ f(x) exp(-ikx) dx; decomposes a function into its frequency components; inverse: f(x) = (1/2π) ∫ f̃(k) exp(ikx) dk.
physics.residue theorem: ∮_C f(z) dz = 2πi * sum(residues inside C); allows evaluation of real integrals by closing contours in the complex plane.
physics.levi-civita tensor: ε_{ijk} = +1 (even permutation), -1 (odd), 0 (repeated index); encodes cross products and determinants in index notation.
physics.plasma: A quasi-neutral ionized gas in which collective electromagnetic effects dominate; the fourth state of matter, comprising most of the visible universe.
physics.debye length: λ_D = sqrt(ε_0 k_B T_e / (n_e e^2)); the characteristic length over which charge imbalances are screened by rearrangement of free charges in a plasma.
physics.plasma frequency: ω_p = sqrt(n_e e^2 / (ε_0 m_e)); the natural oscillation frequency of electron density fluctuations; waves with ω < ω_p cannot propagate in the plasma.
physics.magnetic pressure: P_B = B^2/(2μ_0); the isotropic pressure exerted by a magnetic field; the ratio of plasma to magnetic pressure is β = P/(B^2/2μ_0).
physics.alfvén speed: v_A = B/sqrt(μ_0 ρ); the characteristic speed of MHD Alfvén waves propagating along magnetic field lines in a conducting fluid.
physics.vlasov equation: The collisionless Boltzmann equation ∂f/∂t + v·∇f + (q/m)(E+v×B)·∂f/∂v = 0 governing the distribution function f(r,v,t) of a plasma.
physics.navier-stokes equation: ρ(∂v/∂t + v·∇v) = -∇P + μ∇^2v + f; governs the velocity field of a viscous incompressible Newtonian fluid.
